- 2025-05-06: Updated retention (400 days SIEM searchable + 7 year immutable archive) and added fail-closed guidance for audit pipeline outages.

source.title: Audit Event Schema v2 (Canonical) + SIEM Mapping (Splunk/Datadog)
source.author: Marcus Lin
source.body:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

## 2. Schema overview
Audit Event Schema v2 is a **structured JSON object** emitted by Redwood services into the audit pipeline. Exporters deliver events **at-least-once**; downstream systems must deduplicate using `event_id`.

**Top-level rules (MUST/SHOULD)**
- Every event MUST include: `schema_version`, `event_id`, `occurred_at`, `category`, `action`, `outcome`, `tenant`, `actor`, `resource`.
- `event_id` MUST be stable across retries/replays.
- Timestamps MUST be RFC3339 with milliseconds (UTC), e.g. `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z`.
- Field names are **snake_case**.
- Free-form payload blobs MUST NOT be included. If additional fields are needed, they must be reviewed via the PII/sensitive-fields checklist.

---

## 3. Canonical JSON shape (v2)
The schema is expressed below in a descriptive form (not a strict JSON Schema document).

```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
  "received_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.612Z",

  "category": "iam",
  "action": "api_key.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",

  "tenant": {
    "organization_id": "org_9f2c...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_1a2b...",
    "deployment_mode": "hosted"
  },

  "actor": {
    "type": "user",
    "id": "user_123",
    "display_name": "Avery Chen",
    "email": "redacted",
    "ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 ..."
  },

  "resource": {
    "type": "api_key",
    "id": "key_456",
    "display_name": "Prod key",
    "parent_type": "workspace",
    "parent_id": "ws_1a2b..."
  },

  "request": {
    "request_id": "req_01J1M5...",
    "trace_id": "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736",
    "route": "POST /v1/api-keys",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  },

  "changes": {
    "operation": "create",
    "fields_changed": ["scopes", "name"],
    "redaction": "applied"
  },

  "reason": {
    "code": "none",
    "message": ""
  }
}
```

Notes:
- Nested objects (`tenant`, `actor`, `resource`, `request`) are **expected** and are part of the canonical schema.
- `email` is typically **redacted** in exports; see Section 7.

---

## 4. Field reference (v2)

### 4.1 Required fields
| Field | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| schema_version | string | Version of schema; currently `2.0`. | `2.0` |
| event_id | string | Globally unique, stable identifier. Use ULID (preferred) or UUIDv7. | `01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G` |
| occurred_at | string (RFC3339) | When the audited action occurred. | `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z` |
| category | string | Event category taxonomy (see Section 5). | `iam` |
| action | string | Stable action name (see Section 5). | `rbac.role.updated` |
| outcome | string | `success` or `failure`. | `failure` |
| tenant | object | Tenant context (org/workspace + mode). | `{...}` |
| actor | object | Who/what performed the action. | `{...}` |
| resource | object | Object affected (type/id). | `{...}` |

### 4.2 Optional but strongly recommended
| Field | Type | When to include | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| received_at | string (RFC3339) | When event entered the audit pipeline | Useful to compute ingestion delay; do not confuse with exporter delay |
| severity | string | When a rough severity is useful | Allowed values: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (default `info`) |
| request | object | When there is an API/UI request context | Enables correlation with request logs and traces |
| changes | object | When a mutation occurs | Avoid including old/new values for sensitive fields |
| reason | object | When outcome is failure or blocked | `code` should be enumerated where possible |
| correlation_id | string | When linking a series of actions | E.g., provisioning workflow id |

### 4.3 `tenant` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| organization_id | string | YES | Redwood organization identifier |
| workspace_id | string | YES (most events) | Workspace identifier; if not applicable use `ws_unknown` and document why |
| deployment_mode | string | YES | `hosted` or `dedicated` (beta) |
| dedicated_cluster_id | string | NO | Present for Dedicated where applicable |

### 4.4 `actor` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | `user`, `service_account`, `system` |
| id | string | YES | Stable id for the actor |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted in exports |
| email | string | NO | Generally redacted/minimized; see Section 7 |
| ip | string | NO | Source IP when available and permitted |
| user_agent | string | NO | User agent for UI/API calls when available |
| auth_method | string | NO | `password`, `sso`, `api_key`, `oauth`, `session` |
| role | string | NO | Snapshot of primary role at time of action (avoid large arrays) |

### 4.5 `resource` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | Resource type taxonomy (examples below) |
| id | string | YES | Stable id of the resource |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted |
| parent_type | string | NO | Typically `workspace` or `organization` |
| parent_id | string | NO | Parent identifier |

Common resource types (non-exhaustive):
- `workspace`, `organization`, `user`, `service_account`
- `api_key`, `rbac_role`, `rbac_policy`, `permission`
- `model`, `model_version`, `deployment`, `endpoint`
- `audit_export_config`, `audit_export_destination`
- `secret`, `kms_key_ref` (references only; no raw secrets)

### 4.6 `request` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| request_id | string | NO | Redwood request id for the control plane/API gateway |
| trace_id | string | NO | Distributed trace id (W3C or equivalent) |
| route | string | NO | Normalized route, e.g. `POST /v1/audit/exports` |
| region | string | NO | `us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`, etc. |
| client | string | NO | `console`, `sdk`, `terraform`, `api` |

### 4.7 `changes` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| operation | string | NO | `create`, `update`, `delete`, `rotate`, `enable`, `disable` |
| fields_changed | array(string) | NO | Field names changed; do not include values |
| redaction | string | NO | `none`, `applied`, `not_applicable` |

### 4.8 `reason` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| code | string | NO | Machine-friendly failure/block reason (`auth_failed`, `permission_denied`, `validation_error`, `rate_limited`) |
| message | string | NO | Short message; MUST NOT include sensitive data |

---

## 5. Taxonomy: categories + action naming

### 5.1 Category taxonomy (v2)
Category is used for filtering, routing, and default dashboards.

Allowed categories (beta):
- `authn` (authentication) – login, SSO, session issuance/refresh
- `authz` (authorization) – permission checks denied/allowed (use selectively; avoid high volume)
- `iam` – users, service accounts, API keys, RBAC role/policy changes
- `admin` – tenant/workspace settings, enterprise controls
- `model` – model onboarding, model version changes, routing policy changes
- `deployment` – endpoint creation/updates, dedicated capacity settings
- `security` – security-sensitive settings (KMS refs, allowlists, security posture)
- `audit_export` – audit export configuration lifecycle (create/update/pause/resume/rotate)
- `billing` – plan changes, invoice-related admin actions (no payment instrument details)
- `data_access` – access to sensitive data sets (only when explicitly approved)

### 5.2 Action naming conventions
Action is a stable string; it MUST NOT change without a compatibility plan.

**Format**: `domain.object.verb[.qualifier]`
- Examples: `iam.api_key.created`, `rbac.role.updated`, `audit_export.destination.tested`, `authn.login.succeeded`

**Verb guidance**
- Prefer: `created`, `updated`, `deleted`, `enabled`, `disabled`, `rotated`, `tested`, `paused`, `resumed`, `promoted`, `rolled_back`
- Avoid ambiguous verbs: `changed`, `modified`

**Outcome guidance**
- `success`: action completed as intended
- `failure`: action attempted but failed (include `reason.code`)

---

## 6. Examples (recommended canonical events)

### 6.1 Console login succeeded (authn)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A2K9D0K3J6QJ6K2C7Y9JQH",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T09:04:11.120Z",
  "category": "authn",
  "action": "authn.login.succeeded",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "ip": "203.0.113.10", "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0...", "auth_method": "sso"},
  "resource": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "parent_type": "organization", "parent_id": "org_acme"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A2...", "route": "POST /console/login", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.2 RBAC role updated (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A3B3M7W8B2VY1J3W2Z8JQ2",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T10:21:55.009Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "rbac.role.updated",
  "outcome": "success",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "dedicated", "dedicated_cluster_id": "ded_02h9..."},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_11", "display_name": "Casey Nguyen", "ip": "198.51.100.22"},
  "resource": {"type": "rbac_role", "id": "role_admin", "display_name": "Workspace Admin", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_prod"},
  "changes": {"operation": "update", "fields_changed": ["permissions"], "redaction": "not_applicable"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A3...", "route": "PATCH /v1/rbac/roles/{id}", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "api"}
}
```

### 6.3 Audit export destination created (audit_export)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B0NQ7C4N0W5R2P8T6J3Z6C",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T18:40:02.441Z",
  "category": "audit_export",
  "action": "audit_export.destination.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_7", "display_name": "Morgan Reyes", "ip": "203.0.113.80"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_destination", "id": "dest_splunk_01", "display_name": "Splunk HEC (prod)", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "changes": {"operation": "create", "fields_changed": ["destination_type", "endpoint_host", "index", "sourcetype"], "redaction": "applied"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B0...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/destinations", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.4 Permission denied attempting to rotate credentials (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B1Q9S6T3Q7X4Q8H1K5F8Z9",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T19:01:19.772Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "audit_export.credentials.rotated",
  "outcome": "failure",
  "severity": "warn",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_55", "display_name": "Taylor Singh", "ip": "203.0.113.81"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_config", "id": "aec_92", "display_name": "Security exports", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "reason": {"code": "permission_denied", "message": "Actor lacks audit_export.manage_secrets"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B1...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/{id}/rotate-credentials", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

---

## 7. PII minimization + sensitive fields (summary)
Full policy is maintained in: **PII Redaction and Sensitive Fields Policy for Audit Events**.

### 7.1 Allowed fields (typical)
- Stable identifiers: `organization_id`, `workspace_id`, `actor.id`, `resource.id`
- Limited network identifiers: `actor.ip` (when available and policy allows)
- Client metadata: `user_agent` (when available), but avoid embedding full device fingerprints

### 7.2 Prohibited fields
- Secrets or credentials (tokens, API keys, passwords, session cookies)
- Full request/response bodies
- Payment instrument details
- Customer prompt/completion content

### 7.3 Redaction rules (common)
- `actor.email`: redacted by default in exported events unless explicitly approved for a compliance use case.
- `resource.display_name`: may be redacted if it can contain sensitive customer content (teams must treat names as potentially sensitive).
- `reason.message`: MUST be short and non-sensitive; prefer `reason.code`.

---

## 8. SIEM mapping guidance
This section provides recommended mappings for **Splunk** and **Datadog**. The canonical event must remain intact; mappings are additive/derivative.

### 8.1 General mapping principles
- Preserve canonical JSON under a stable key (e.g., `event` or `redwood.audit`).
- Promote a small set of fields to top-level SIEM fields for searchability:
  - time (`occurred_at`)
  - identifiers (`event_id`, `organization_id`, `workspace_id`)
  - taxonomy (`category`, `action`, `outcome`)
  - actor (`actor.type`, `actor.id`, `actor.ip`)
  - resource (`resource.type`, `resource.id`)
- Control cardinality:
  - Avoid promoting `display_name` to indexed fields.
  - Avoid high-cardinality arrays.

---

## 9. Splunk mapping (HEC + CIM-esque conventions)

### 9.1 Recommended Splunk HEC envelope
When delivering to Splunk HEC, the exporter SHOULD format each event as:

```json
{
  "time": 1745130764.391,
  "host": "redwood",
  "source": "redwood:audit",
  "sourcetype": "redwood:audit:v2",
  "index": "<customer-configured>",
  "event": {
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "schema_version": "2.0",
    "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_...", "workspace_id": "ws_...", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
    "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_...", "ip": "203.0.113.17"},
    "resource": {"type": "api_key", "id": "key_..."},
    "request": {"request_id": "req_...", "route": "POST /v1/api-keys", "region": "us-east-1"}
  },
  "fields": {
    "vendor": "redwood",
    "product": "redwood-inference",
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "org_id": "org_...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_...",
    "actor_type": "user",
    "actor_id": "user_...",
    "src_ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "resource_type": "api_key",
    "resource_id": "key_...",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  }
}
```

Notes:
- `time` MUST correspond to `occurred_at` (not `received_at`).
- `fields` is used to promote search-friendly keys without forcing users to query nested JSON.
- Customers may configure `index`, `source`, and `sourcetype` during beta. Recommended default `sourcetype=redwood:audit:v2`.

### 9.2 CIM-style field mapping (guidance)
Splunk CIM varies by data model. For audit/admin activity searches, map as follows:

| Canonical field | Suggested Splunk field | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| occurred_at | _time | Derived from HEC `time` |
| event_id | event_id | Stable dedup key |
| category | tag / category | Consider `tag=redwood,audit,<category>` |
| action | action | Keep stable string |
| outcome | outcome | `success`/`failure` |
| actor.id | user / user_id | Use `user_id` if available in customer conventions |
| actor.ip | src / src_ip | Avoid if policy disallows |
| resource.type | object_category / object_type | Prefer `object_type` |
| resource.id | object_id | |
| tenant.organization_id | org_id | |
| tenant.workspace_id | workspace_id | |
| request.route | uri_path | Optional; may increase cardinality |
| request.request_id | request_id | Useful for correlation |

Recommended tags:
- `tag=redwood`
- `tag=audit`
- `tag=<category>` (e.g., `iam`, `authn`, `audit_export`)

### 9.3 Splunk dedup recommendation
Because delivery is **at-least-once**, customers SHOULD deduplicate using `event_id`. Example SPL:
- `... | dedup event_id`

---

## 10. Datadog Logs mapping (facets + tags)

### 10.1 Recommended Datadog log structure
Deliver canonical JSON as the log body and promote select attributes.

Preferred Datadog attributes:
- `ddsource`: `redwood`
- `service`: `audit-log-export` (exporter) or `control-plane` (if ingesting directly)
- `status`: map from `severity` (`info`, `warn`, `error`)

Example Datadog log event (conceptual):
```json
{
  "ddsource": "redwood",
  "service": "audit-log-export",
  "message": "redwood audit event v2",
  "timestamp": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",

  "redwood.audit.event_id": "01J1M5...",
  "redwood.audit.schema_version": "2.0",
  "redwood.audit.category": "iam",
  "redwood.audit.action": "api_key.created",
  "redwood.audit.outcome": "success",

  "redwood.org_id": "org_...",
  "redwood.workspace_id": "ws_...",
  "redwood.deployment_mode": "hosted",

  "usr.id": "user_123",
  "usr.type": "user",
  "network.client.ip": "203.0.113.17",
  "http.useragent": "Mozilla/5.0...",

  "redwood.resource.type": "api_key",
  "redwood.resource.id": "key_456",

  "redwood.request.id": "req_...",
  "redwood.trace_id": "4bf92f...",
  "redwood.region": "us-east-1",

  "tags": [
    "vendor:redwood",
    "product:redwood-inference",
    "category:iam",
    "action:api_key.created",
    "outcome:success",
    "workspace:ws_...",
    "org:org_...",
    "region:us-east-1"
  ],

  "event": {"<full canonical event>": "..."}
}
```

### 10.2 Datadog facet recommendations
Promote these as facets for common queries:
- `redwood.audit.category`
- `redwood.audit.action`
- `redwood.audit.outcome`
- `redwood.org_id`
- `redwood.workspace_id`
- `usr.id` and `network.client.ip` (subject to privacy policy)
- `redwood.resource.type` and `redwood.resource.id`

Avoid promoting:
- `actor.display_name`
- `resource.display_name`
- `request.route` (unless customers explicitly want it; can explode cardinality)

---

## 11. Versioning + compatibility

### 11.1 Schema versioning policy
- `schema_version` MUST be present and parseable.
- Minor additions (new optional fields) may be introduced under `2.x` without breaking.
- Breaking changes require a major bump (e.g., `3.0`) and an export compatibility plan.

### 11.2 Action/category stability
- `category` and `action` are treated as stable contracts for:
  - customer filters
  - SIEM parsers
  - compliance evidence queries
- Renames are strongly discouraged. If required, we must:
  - emit both old and new action for a defined transition window OR
  - provide a documented alias mapping and update exporter-side transforms.

---

## 12. Checklist for adding a new audit event type
Before adding a new event type (new `action`):
1. Choose `category` from Section 5 and a stable `action` string.
2. Confirm required fields are present (Section 4.1).
3. Ensure no prohibited fields are included (Section 7.2).
4. If including `actor.ip` or `user_agent`, confirm collection is permitted and expected.
5. Add to:
   - category/action allowlist (filtering model)
   - SIEM mapping notes if a new common field is introduced
6. Request review from:
   - Security Engineering (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei)
   - Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park) if new tenant context fields are needed

---

## 13. Appendix: Reason codes (initial set)
Teams SHOULD use these `reason.code` values for failures/blocks when applicable:
- `permission_denied`
- `auth_failed`
- `validation_error`
- `not_found`
- `conflict`
- `rate_limited`
- `upstream_unavailable`
- `policy_blocked`

If adding a new reason code, update this appendix and ensure it is documented in customer-facing FAQ language where needed.

title: Audit Event Schema v2 (Canonical) + SIEM Mapping (Splunk/Datadog)
preview:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

## 2. Schema overview
Audit Event Schema v2 is a **structured JSON object** emitted by Redwood services into the audit pipeline. Exporters deliver events **at-least-once**; downstream systems must deduplicate using `event_id`.

**Top-level rules (MUST/SHOULD)**
- Every event MUST include: `schema_version`, `event_id`, `occurred_at`, `category`, `action`, `outcome`, `tenant`, `actor`, `resource`.
- `event_id` MUST be stable across retries/replays.
- Timestamps MUST be RFC3339 with milliseconds (UTC), e.g. `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z`.
- Field names are **snake_case**.
- Free-form payload blobs MUST NOT be included. If additional fields are needed, they must be reviewed via the PII/sensitive-fields checklist.

---

## 3. Canonical JSON shape (v2)
The schema is expressed below in a descriptive form (not a strict JSON Schema document).

```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
  "received_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.612Z",

  "category": "iam",
  "action": "api_key.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",

  "tenant": {
    "organization_id": "org_9f2c...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_1a2b...",
    "deployment_mode": "hosted"
  },

  "actor": {
    "type": "user",
    "id": "user_123",
    "display_name": "Avery Chen",
    "email": "redacted",
    "ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 ..."
  },

  "resource": {
    "type": "api_key",
    "id": "key_456",
    "display_name": "Prod key",
    "parent_type": "workspace",
    "parent_id": "ws_1a2b..."
  },

  "request": {
    "request_id": "req_01J1M5...",
    "trace_id": "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736",
    "route": "POST /v1/api-keys",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  },

  "changes": {
    "operation": "create",
    "fields_changed": ["scopes", "name"],
    "redaction": "applied"
  },

  "reason": {
    "code": "none",
    "message": ""
  }
}
```

Notes:
- Nested objects (`tenant`, `actor`, `resource`, `request`) are **expected** and are part of the canonical schema.
- `email` is typically **redacted** in exports; see Section 7.

---

## 4. Field reference (v2)

### 4.1 Required fields
| Field | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| schema_version | string | Version of schema; currently `2.0`. | `2.0` |
| event_id | string | Globally unique, stable identifier. Use ULID (preferred) or UUIDv7. | `01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G` |
| occurred_at | string (RFC3339) | When the audited action occurred. | `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z` |
| category | string | Event category taxonomy (see Section 5). | `iam` |
| action | string | Stable action name (see Section 5). | `rbac.role.updated` |
| outcome | string | `success` or `failure`. | `failure` |
| tenant | object | Tenant context (org/workspace + mode). | `{...}` |
| actor | object | Who/what performed the action. | `{...}` |
| resource | object | Object affected (type/id). | `{...}` |

### 4.2 Optional but strongly recommended
| Field | Type | When to include | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| received_at | string (RFC3339) | When event entered the audit pipeline | Useful to compute ingestion delay; do not confuse with exporter delay |
| severity | string | When a rough severity is useful | Allowed values: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (default `info`) |
| request | object | When there is an API/UI request context | Enables correlation with request logs and traces |
| changes | object | When a mutation occurs | Avoid including old/new values for sensitive fields |
| reason | object | When outcome is failure or blocked | `code` should be enumerated where possible |
| correlation_id | string | When linking a series of actions | E.g., provisioning workflow id |

### 4.3 `tenant` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| organization_id | string | YES | Redwood organization identifier |
| workspace_id | string | YES (most events) | Workspace identifier; if not applicable use `ws_unknown` and document why |
| deployment_mode | string | YES | `hosted` or `dedicated` (beta) |
| dedicated_cluster_id | string | NO | Present for Dedicated where applicable |

### 4.4 `actor` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | `user`, `service_account`, `system` |
| id | string | YES | Stable id for the actor |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted in exports |
| email | string | NO | Generally redacted/minimized; see Section 7 |
| ip | string | NO | Source IP when available and permitted |
| user_agent | string | NO | User agent for UI/API calls when available |
| auth_method | string | NO | `password`, `sso`, `api_key`, `oauth`, `session` |
| role | string | NO | Snapshot of primary role at time of action (avoid large arrays) |

### 4.5 `resource` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | Resource type taxonomy (examples below) |
| id | string | YES | Stable id of the resource |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted |
| parent_type | string | NO | Typically `workspace` or `organization` |
| parent_id | string | NO | Parent identifier |

Common resource types (non-exhaustive):
- `workspace`, `organization`, `user`, `service_account`
- `api_key`, `rbac_role`, `rbac_policy`, `permission`
- `model`, `model_version`, `deployment`, `endpoint`
- `audit_export_config`, `audit_export_destination`
- `secret`, `kms_key_ref` (references only; no raw secrets)

### 4.6 `request` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| request_id | string | NO | Redwood request id for the control plane/API gateway |
| trace_id | string | NO | Distributed trace id (W3C or equivalent) |
| route | string | NO | Normalized route, e.g. `POST /v1/audit/exports` |
| region | string | NO | `us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`, etc. |
| client | string | NO | `console`, `sdk`, `terraform`, `api` |

### 4.7 `changes` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| operation | string | NO | `create`, `update`, `delete`, `rotate`, `enable`, `disable` |
| fields_changed | array(string) | NO | Field names changed; do not include values |
| redaction | string | NO | `none`, `applied`, `not_applicable` |

### 4.8 `reason` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| code | string | NO | Machine-friendly failure/block reason (`auth_failed`, `permission_denied`, `validation_error`, `rate_limited`) |
| message | string | NO | Short message; MUST NOT include sensitive data |

---

## 5. Taxonomy: categories + action naming

### 5.1 Category taxonomy (v2)
Category is used for filtering, routing, and default dashboards.

Allowed categories (beta):
- `authn` (authentication) – login, SSO, session issuance/refresh
- `authz` (authorization) – permission checks denied/allowed (use selectively; avoid high volume)
- `iam` – users, service accounts, API keys, RBAC role/policy changes
- `admin` – tenant/workspace settings, enterprise controls
- `model` – model onboarding, model version changes, routing policy changes
- `deployment` – endpoint creation/updates, dedicated capacity settings
- `security` – security-sensitive settings (KMS refs, allowlists, security posture)
- `audit_export` – audit export configuration lifecycle (create/update/pause/resume/rotate)
- `billing` – plan changes, invoice-related admin actions (no payment instrument details)
- `data_access` – access to sensitive data sets (only when explicitly approved)

### 5.2 Action naming conventions
Action is a stable string; it MUST NOT change without a compatibility plan.

**Format**: `domain.object.verb[.qualifier]`
- Examples: `iam.api_key.created`, `rbac.role.updated`, `audit_export.destination.tested`, `authn.login.succeeded`

**Verb guidance**
- Prefer: `created`, `updated`, `deleted`, `enabled`, `disabled`, `rotated`, `tested`, `paused`, `resumed`, `promoted`, `rolled_back`
- Avoid ambiguous verbs: `changed`, `modified`

**Outcome guidance**
- `success`: action completed as intended
- `failure`: action attempted but failed (include `reason.code`)

---

## 6. Examples (recommended canonical events)

### 6.1 Console login succeeded (authn)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A2K9D0K3J6QJ6K2C7Y9JQH",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T09:04:11.120Z",
  "category": "authn",
  "action": "authn.login.succeeded",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "ip": "203.0.113.10", "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0...", "auth_method": "sso"},
  "resource": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "parent_type": "organization", "parent_id": "org_acme"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A2...", "route": "POST /console/login", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.2 RBAC role updated (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A3B3M7W8B2VY1J3W2Z8JQ2",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T10:21:55.009Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "rbac.role.updated",
  "outcome": "success",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "dedicated", "dedicated_cluster_id": "ded_02h9..."},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_11", "display_name": "Casey Nguyen", "ip": "198.51.100.22"},
  "resource": {"type": "rbac_role", "id": "role_admin", "display_name": "Workspace Admin", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_prod"},
  "changes": {"operation": "update", "fields_changed": ["permissions"], "redaction": "not_applicable"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A3...", "route": "PATCH /v1/rbac/roles/{id}", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "api"}
}
```

### 6.3 Audit export destination created (audit_export)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B0NQ7C4N0W5R2P8T6J3Z6C",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T18:40:02.441Z",
  "category": "audit_export",
  "action": "audit_export.destination.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_7", "display_name": "Morgan Reyes", "ip": "203.0.113.80"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_destination", "id": "dest_splunk_01", "display_name": "Splunk HEC (prod)", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "changes": {"operation": "create", "fields_changed": ["destination_type", "endpoint_host", "index", "sourcetype"], "redaction": "applied"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B0...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/destinations", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.4 Permission denied attempting to rotate credentials (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B1Q9S6T3Q7X4Q8H1K5F8Z9",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T19:01:19.772Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "audit_export.credentials.rotated",
  "outcome": "failure",
  "severity": "warn",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_55", "display_name": "Taylor Singh", "ip": "203.0.113.81"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_config", "id": "aec_92", "display_name": "Security exports", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "reason": {"code": "permission_denied", "message": "Actor lacks audit_export.manage_secrets"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B1...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/{id}/rotate-credentials", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

---

## 7. PII minimization + sensitive fields (summary)
Full policy is maintained in: **PII Redaction and Sensitive Fields Policy for Audit Events**.

### 7.1 Allowed fields (typical)
- Stable identifiers: `organization_id`, `workspace_id`, `actor.id`, `resource.id`
- Limited network identifiers: `actor.ip` (when available and policy allows)
- Client metadata: `user_agent` (when available), but avoid embedding full device fingerprints

### 7.2 Prohibited fields
- Secrets or credentials (tokens, API keys, passwords, session cookies)
- Full request/response bodies
- Payment instrument details
- Customer prompt/completion content

### 7.3 Redaction rules (common)
- `actor.email`: redacted by default in exported events unless explicitly approved for a compliance use case.
- `resource.display_name`: may be redacted if it can contain sensitive customer content (teams must treat names as potentially sensitive).
- `reason.message`: MUST be short and non-sensitive; prefer `reason.code`.

---

## 8. SIEM mapping guidance
This section provides recommended mappings for **Splunk** and **Datadog**. The canonical event must remain intact; mappings are additive/derivative.

### 8.1 General mapping principles
- Preserve canonical JSON under a stable key (e.g., `event` or `redwood.audit`).
- Promote a small set of fields to top-level SIEM fields for searchability:
  - time (`occurred_at`)
  - identifiers (`event_id`, `organization_id`, `workspace_id`)
  - taxonomy (`category`, `action`, `outcome`)
  - actor (`actor.type`, `actor.id`, `actor.ip`)
  - resource (`resource.type`, `resource.id`)
- Control cardinality:
  - Avoid promoting `display_name` to indexed fields.
  - Avoid high-cardinality arrays.

---

## 9. Splunk mapping (HEC + CIM-esque conventions)

### 9.1 Recommended Splunk HEC envelope
When delivering to Splunk HEC, the exporter SHOULD format each event as:

```json
{
  "time": 1745130764.391,
  "host": "redwood",
  "source": "redwood:audit",
  "sourcetype": "redwood:audit:v2",
  "index": "<customer-configured>",
  "event": {
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "schema_version": "2.0",
    "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_...", "workspace_id": "ws_...", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
    "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_...", "ip": "203.0.113.17"},
    "resource": {"type": "api_key", "id": "key_..."},
    "request": {"request_id": "req_...", "route": "POST /v1/api-keys", "region": "us-east-1"}
  },
  "fields": {
    "vendor": "redwood",
    "product": "redwood-inference",
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "org_id": "org_...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_...",
    "actor_type": "user",
    "actor_id": "user_...",
    "src_ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "resource_type": "api_key",
    "resource_id": "key_...",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  }
}
```

Notes:
- `time` MUST correspond to `occurred_at` (not `received_at`).
- `fields` is used to promote search-friendly keys without forcing users to query nested JSON.
- Customers may configure `index`, `source`, and `sourcetype` during beta. Recommended default `sourcetype=redwood:audit:v2`.

### 9.2 CIM-style field mapping (guidance)
Splunk CIM varies by data model. For audit/admin activity searches, map as follows:

| Canonical field | Suggested Splunk field | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| occurred_at | _time | Derived from HEC `time` |
| event_id | event_id | Stable dedup key |
| category | tag / category | Consider `tag=redwood,audit,<category>` |
| action | action | Keep stable string |
| outcome | outcome | `success`/`failure` |
| actor.id | user / user_id | Use `user_id` if available in customer conventions |
| actor.ip | src / src_ip | Avoid if policy disallows |
| resource.type | object_category / object_type | Prefer `object_type` |
| resource.id | object_id | |
| tenant.organization_id | org_id | |
| tenant.workspace_id | workspace_id | |
| request.route | uri_path | Optional; may increase cardinality |
| request.request_id | request_id | Useful for correlation |

Recommended tags:
- `tag=redwood`
- `tag=audit`
- `tag=<category>` (e.g., `iam`, `authn`, `audit_export`)

### 9.3 Splunk dedup recommendation
Because delivery is **at-least-once**, customers SHOULD deduplicate using `event_id`. Example SPL:
- `... | dedup event_id`

---

## 10. Datadog Logs mapping (facets + tags)

### 10.1 Recommended Datadog log structure
Deliver canonical JSON as the log body and promote select attributes.

Preferred Datadog attributes:
- `ddsource`: `redwood`
- `service`: `audit-log-export` (exporter) or `control-plane` (if ingesting directly)
- `status`: map from `severity` (`info`, `warn`, `error`)

Example Datadog log event (conceptual):
```json
{
  "ddsource": "redwood",
  "service": "audit-log-export",
  "message": "redwood audit event v2",
  "timestamp": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",

  "redwood.audit.event_id": "01J1M5...",
  "redwood.audit.schema_version": "2.0",
  "redwood.audit.category": "iam",
  "redwood.audit.action": "api_key.created",
  "redwood.audit.outcome": "success",

  "redwood.org_id": "org_...",
  "redwood.workspace_id": "ws_...",
  "redwood.deployment_mode": "hosted",

  "usr.id": "user_123",
  "usr.type": "user",
  "network.client.ip": "203.0.113.17",
  "http.useragent": "Mozilla/5.0...",

  "redwood.resource.type": "api_key",
  "redwood.resource.id": "key_456",

  "redwood.request.id": "req_...",
  "redwood.trace_id": "4bf92f...",
  "redwood.region": "us-east-1",

  "tags": [
    "vendor:redwood",
    "product:redwood-inference",
    "category:iam",
    "action:api_key.created",
    "outcome:success",
    "workspace:ws_...",
    "org:org_...",
    "region:us-east-1"
  ],

  "event": {"<full canonical event>": "..."}
}
```

### 10.2 Datadog facet recommendations
Promote these as facets for common queries:
- `redwood.audit.category`
- `redwood.audit.action`
- `redwood.audit.outcome`
- `redwood.org_id`
- `redwood.workspace_id`
- `usr.id` and `network.client.ip` (subject to privacy policy)
- `redwood.resource.type` and `redwood.resource.id`

Avoid promoting:
- `actor.display_name`
- `resource.display_name`
- `request.route` (unless customers explicitly want it; can explode cardinality)

---

## 11. Versioning + compatibility

### 11.1 Schema versioning policy
- `schema_version` MUST be present and parseable.
- Minor additions (new optional fields) may be introduced under `2.x` without breaking.
- Breaking changes require a major bump (e.g., `3.0`) and an export compatibility plan.

### 11.2 Action/category stability
- `category` and `action` are treated as stable contracts for:
  - customer filters
  - SIEM parsers
  - compliance evidence queries
- Renames are strongly discouraged. If required, we must:
  - emit both old and new action for a defined transition window OR
  - provide a documented alias mapping and update exporter-side transforms.

---

## 12. Checklist for adding a new audit event type
Before adding a new event type (new `action`):
1. Choose `category` from Section 5 and a stable `action` string.
2. Confirm required fields are present (Section 4.1).
3. Ensure no prohibited fields are included (Section 7.2).
4. If including `actor.ip` or `user_agent`, confirm collection is permitted and expected.
5. Add to:
   - category/action allowlist (filtering model)
   - SIEM mapping notes if a new common field is introduced
6. Request review from:
   - Security Engineering (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei)
   - Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park) if new tenant context fields are needed

---

## 13. Appendix: Reason codes (initial set)
Teams SHOULD use these `reason.code` values for failures/blocks when applicable:
- `permission_denied`
- `auth_failed`
- `validation_error`
- `not_found`
- `conflict`
- `rate_limited`
- `upstream_unavailable`
- `policy_blocked`

If adding a new reason code, update this appendix and ensure it is documented in customer-facing FAQ language where needed.
text:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

## 2. Schema overview
Audit Event Schema v2 is a **structured JSON object** emitted by Redwood services into the audit pipeline. Exporters deliver events **at-least-once**; downstream systems must deduplicate using `event_id`.

**Top-level rules (MUST/SHOULD)**
- Every event MUST include: `schema_version`, `event_id`, `occurred_at`, `category`, `action`, `outcome`, `tenant`, `actor`, `resource`.
- `event_id` MUST be stable across retries/replays.
- Timestamps MUST be RFC3339 with milliseconds (UTC), e.g. `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z`.
- Field names are **snake_case**.
- Free-form payload blobs MUST NOT be included. If additional fields are needed, they must be reviewed via the PII/sensitive-fields checklist.

---

## 3. Canonical JSON shape (v2)
The schema is expressed below in a descriptive form (not a strict JSON Schema document).

```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
  "received_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.612Z",

  "category": "iam",
  "action": "api_key.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",

  "tenant": {
    "organization_id": "org_9f2c...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_1a2b...",
    "deployment_mode": "hosted"
  },

  "actor": {
    "type": "user",
    "id": "user_123",
    "display_name": "Avery Chen",
    "email": "redacted",
    "ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 ..."
  },

  "resource": {
    "type": "api_key",
    "id": "key_456",
    "display_name": "Prod key",
    "parent_type": "workspace",
    "parent_id": "ws_1a2b..."
  },

  "request": {
    "request_id": "req_01J1M5...",
    "trace_id": "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736",
    "route": "POST /v1/api-keys",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  },

  "changes": {
    "operation": "create",
    "fields_changed": ["scopes", "name"],
    "redaction": "applied"
  },

  "reason": {
    "code": "none",
    "message": ""
  }
}
```

Notes:
- Nested objects (`tenant`, `actor`, `resource`, `request`) are **expected** and are part of the canonical schema.
- `email` is typically **redacted** in exports; see Section 7.

---

## 4. Field reference (v2)

### 4.1 Required fields
| Field | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| schema_version | string | Version of schema; currently `2.0`. | `2.0` |
| event_id | string | Globally unique, stable identifier. Use ULID (preferred) or UUIDv7. | `01J1M5P8Z0R3Z8W0VQ8D0S0Y3G` |
| occurred_at | string (RFC3339) | When the audited action occurred. | `2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z` |
| category | string | Event category taxonomy (see Section 5). | `iam` |
| action | string | Stable action name (see Section 5). | `rbac.role.updated` |
| outcome | string | `success` or `failure`. | `failure` |
| tenant | object | Tenant context (org/workspace + mode). | `{...}` |
| actor | object | Who/what performed the action. | `{...}` |
| resource | object | Object affected (type/id). | `{...}` |

### 4.2 Optional but strongly recommended
| Field | Type | When to include | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| received_at | string (RFC3339) | When event entered the audit pipeline | Useful to compute ingestion delay; do not confuse with exporter delay |
| severity | string | When a rough severity is useful | Allowed values: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (default `info`) |
| request | object | When there is an API/UI request context | Enables correlation with request logs and traces |
| changes | object | When a mutation occurs | Avoid including old/new values for sensitive fields |
| reason | object | When outcome is failure or blocked | `code` should be enumerated where possible |
| correlation_id | string | When linking a series of actions | E.g., provisioning workflow id |

### 4.3 `tenant` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| organization_id | string | YES | Redwood organization identifier |
| workspace_id | string | YES (most events) | Workspace identifier; if not applicable use `ws_unknown` and document why |
| deployment_mode | string | YES | `hosted` or `dedicated` (beta) |
| dedicated_cluster_id | string | NO | Present for Dedicated where applicable |

### 4.4 `actor` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | `user`, `service_account`, `system` |
| id | string | YES | Stable id for the actor |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted in exports |
| email | string | NO | Generally redacted/minimized; see Section 7 |
| ip | string | NO | Source IP when available and permitted |
| user_agent | string | NO | User agent for UI/API calls when available |
| auth_method | string | NO | `password`, `sso`, `api_key`, `oauth`, `session` |
| role | string | NO | Snapshot of primary role at time of action (avoid large arrays) |

### 4.5 `resource` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| type | string | YES | Resource type taxonomy (examples below) |
| id | string | YES | Stable id of the resource |
| display_name | string | NO | Human-friendly name; may be redacted |
| parent_type | string | NO | Typically `workspace` or `organization` |
| parent_id | string | NO | Parent identifier |

Common resource types (non-exhaustive):
- `workspace`, `organization`, `user`, `service_account`
- `api_key`, `rbac_role`, `rbac_policy`, `permission`
- `model`, `model_version`, `deployment`, `endpoint`
- `audit_export_config`, `audit_export_destination`
- `secret`, `kms_key_ref` (references only; no raw secrets)

### 4.6 `request` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| request_id | string | NO | Redwood request id for the control plane/API gateway |
| trace_id | string | NO | Distributed trace id (W3C or equivalent) |
| route | string | NO | Normalized route, e.g. `POST /v1/audit/exports` |
| region | string | NO | `us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`, etc. |
| client | string | NO | `console`, `sdk`, `terraform`, `api` |

### 4.7 `changes` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| operation | string | NO | `create`, `update`, `delete`, `rotate`, `enable`, `disable` |
| fields_changed | array(string) | NO | Field names changed; do not include values |
| redaction | string | NO | `none`, `applied`, `not_applicable` |

### 4.8 `reason` object
| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| code | string | NO | Machine-friendly failure/block reason (`auth_failed`, `permission_denied`, `validation_error`, `rate_limited`) |
| message | string | NO | Short message; MUST NOT include sensitive data |

---

## 5. Taxonomy: categories + action naming

### 5.1 Category taxonomy (v2)
Category is used for filtering, routing, and default dashboards.

Allowed categories (beta):
- `authn` (authentication) – login, SSO, session issuance/refresh
- `authz` (authorization) – permission checks denied/allowed (use selectively; avoid high volume)
- `iam` – users, service accounts, API keys, RBAC role/policy changes
- `admin` – tenant/workspace settings, enterprise controls
- `model` – model onboarding, model version changes, routing policy changes
- `deployment` – endpoint creation/updates, dedicated capacity settings
- `security` – security-sensitive settings (KMS refs, allowlists, security posture)
- `audit_export` – audit export configuration lifecycle (create/update/pause/resume/rotate)
- `billing` – plan changes, invoice-related admin actions (no payment instrument details)
- `data_access` – access to sensitive data sets (only when explicitly approved)

### 5.2 Action naming conventions
Action is a stable string; it MUST NOT change without a compatibility plan.

**Format**: `domain.object.verb[.qualifier]`
- Examples: `iam.api_key.created`, `rbac.role.updated`, `audit_export.destination.tested`, `authn.login.succeeded`

**Verb guidance**
- Prefer: `created`, `updated`, `deleted`, `enabled`, `disabled`, `rotated`, `tested`, `paused`, `resumed`, `promoted`, `rolled_back`
- Avoid ambiguous verbs: `changed`, `modified`

**Outcome guidance**
- `success`: action completed as intended
- `failure`: action attempted but failed (include `reason.code`)

---

## 6. Examples (recommended canonical events)

### 6.1 Console login succeeded (authn)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A2K9D0K3J6QJ6K2C7Y9JQH",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T09:04:11.120Z",
  "category": "authn",
  "action": "authn.login.succeeded",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "ip": "203.0.113.10", "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0...", "auth_method": "sso"},
  "resource": {"type": "user", "id": "user_82", "display_name": "Jordan Patel", "parent_type": "organization", "parent_id": "org_acme"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A2...", "route": "POST /console/login", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.2 RBAC role updated (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2A3B3M7W8B2VY1J3W2Z8JQ2",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-08T10:21:55.009Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "rbac.role.updated",
  "outcome": "success",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_prod", "deployment_mode": "dedicated", "dedicated_cluster_id": "ded_02h9..."},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_11", "display_name": "Casey Nguyen", "ip": "198.51.100.22"},
  "resource": {"type": "rbac_role", "id": "role_admin", "display_name": "Workspace Admin", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_prod"},
  "changes": {"operation": "update", "fields_changed": ["permissions"], "redaction": "not_applicable"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2A3...", "route": "PATCH /v1/rbac/roles/{id}", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "api"}
}
```

### 6.3 Audit export destination created (audit_export)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B0NQ7C4N0W5R2P8T6J3Z6C",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T18:40:02.441Z",
  "category": "audit_export",
  "action": "audit_export.destination.created",
  "outcome": "success",
  "severity": "info",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_7", "display_name": "Morgan Reyes", "ip": "203.0.113.80"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_destination", "id": "dest_splunk_01", "display_name": "Splunk HEC (prod)", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "changes": {"operation": "create", "fields_changed": ["destination_type", "endpoint_host", "index", "sourcetype"], "redaction": "applied"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B0...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/destinations", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

### 6.4 Permission denied attempting to rotate credentials (iam)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "2.0",
  "event_id": "01J2B1Q9S6T3Q7X4Q8H1K5F8Z9",
  "occurred_at": "2025-04-12T19:01:19.772Z",
  "category": "iam",
  "action": "audit_export.credentials.rotated",
  "outcome": "failure",
  "severity": "warn",
  "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_acme", "workspace_id": "ws_security", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
  "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_55", "display_name": "Taylor Singh", "ip": "203.0.113.81"},
  "resource": {"type": "audit_export_config", "id": "aec_92", "display_name": "Security exports", "parent_type": "workspace", "parent_id": "ws_security"},
  "reason": {"code": "permission_denied", "message": "Actor lacks audit_export.manage_secrets"},
  "request": {"request_id": "req_01J2B1...", "route": "POST /v1/audit/exports/{id}/rotate-credentials", "region": "us-east-1", "client": "console"}
}
```

---

## 7. PII minimization + sensitive fields (summary)
Full policy is maintained in: **PII Redaction and Sensitive Fields Policy for Audit Events**.

### 7.1 Allowed fields (typical)
- Stable identifiers: `organization_id`, `workspace_id`, `actor.id`, `resource.id`
- Limited network identifiers: `actor.ip` (when available and policy allows)
- Client metadata: `user_agent` (when available), but avoid embedding full device fingerprints

### 7.2 Prohibited fields
- Secrets or credentials (tokens, API keys, passwords, session cookies)
- Full request/response bodies
- Payment instrument details
- Customer prompt/completion content

### 7.3 Redaction rules (common)
- `actor.email`: redacted by default in exported events unless explicitly approved for a compliance use case.
- `resource.display_name`: may be redacted if it can contain sensitive customer content (teams must treat names as potentially sensitive).
- `reason.message`: MUST be short and non-sensitive; prefer `reason.code`.

---

## 8. SIEM mapping guidance
This section provides recommended mappings for **Splunk** and **Datadog**. The canonical event must remain intact; mappings are additive/derivative.

### 8.1 General mapping principles
- Preserve canonical JSON under a stable key (e.g., `event` or `redwood.audit`).
- Promote a small set of fields to top-level SIEM fields for searchability:
  - time (`occurred_at`)
  - identifiers (`event_id`, `organization_id`, `workspace_id`)
  - taxonomy (`category`, `action`, `outcome`)
  - actor (`actor.type`, `actor.id`, `actor.ip`)
  - resource (`resource.type`, `resource.id`)
- Control cardinality:
  - Avoid promoting `display_name` to indexed fields.
  - Avoid high-cardinality arrays.

---

## 9. Splunk mapping (HEC + CIM-esque conventions)

### 9.1 Recommended Splunk HEC envelope
When delivering to Splunk HEC, the exporter SHOULD format each event as:

```json
{
  "time": 1745130764.391,
  "host": "redwood",
  "source": "redwood:audit",
  "sourcetype": "redwood:audit:v2",
  "index": "<customer-configured>",
  "event": {
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "schema_version": "2.0",
    "occurred_at": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "tenant": {"organization_id": "org_...", "workspace_id": "ws_...", "deployment_mode": "hosted"},
    "actor": {"type": "user", "id": "user_...", "ip": "203.0.113.17"},
    "resource": {"type": "api_key", "id": "key_..."},
    "request": {"request_id": "req_...", "route": "POST /v1/api-keys", "region": "us-east-1"}
  },
  "fields": {
    "vendor": "redwood",
    "product": "redwood-inference",
    "event_id": "01J1M5...",
    "category": "iam",
    "action": "api_key.created",
    "outcome": "success",
    "org_id": "org_...",
    "workspace_id": "ws_...",
    "actor_type": "user",
    "actor_id": "user_...",
    "src_ip": "203.0.113.17",
    "resource_type": "api_key",
    "resource_id": "key_...",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  }
}
```

Notes:
- `time` MUST correspond to `occurred_at` (not `received_at`).
- `fields` is used to promote search-friendly keys without forcing users to query nested JSON.
- Customers may configure `index`, `source`, and `sourcetype` during beta. Recommended default `sourcetype=redwood:audit:v2`.

### 9.2 CIM-style field mapping (guidance)
Splunk CIM varies by data model. For audit/admin activity searches, map as follows:

| Canonical field | Suggested Splunk field | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| occurred_at | _time | Derived from HEC `time` |
| event_id | event_id | Stable dedup key |
| category | tag / category | Consider `tag=redwood,audit,<category>` |
| action | action | Keep stable string |
| outcome | outcome | `success`/`failure` |
| actor.id | user / user_id | Use `user_id` if available in customer conventions |
| actor.ip | src / src_ip | Avoid if policy disallows |
| resource.type | object_category / object_type | Prefer `object_type` |
| resource.id | object_id | |
| tenant.organization_id | org_id | |
| tenant.workspace_id | workspace_id | |
| request.route | uri_path | Optional; may increase cardinality |
| request.request_id | request_id | Useful for correlation |

Recommended tags:
- `tag=redwood`
- `tag=audit`
- `tag=<category>` (e.g., `iam`, `authn`, `audit_export`)

### 9.3 Splunk dedup recommendation
Because delivery is **at-least-once**, customers SHOULD deduplicate using `event_id`. Example SPL:
- `... | dedup event_id`

---

## 10. Datadog Logs mapping (facets + tags)

### 10.1 Recommended Datadog log structure
Deliver canonical JSON as the log body and promote select attributes.

Preferred Datadog attributes:
- `ddsource`: `redwood`
- `service`: `audit-log-export` (exporter) or `control-plane` (if ingesting directly)
- `status`: map from `severity` (`info`, `warn`, `error`)

Example Datadog log event (conceptual):
```json
{
  "ddsource": "redwood",
  "service": "audit-log-export",
  "message": "redwood audit event v2",
  "timestamp": "2025-04-21T17:12:44.391Z",

  "redwood.audit.event_id": "01J1M5...",
  "redwood.audit.schema_version": "2.0",
  "redwood.audit.category": "iam",
  "redwood.audit.action": "api_key.created",
  "redwood.audit.outcome": "success",

  "redwood.org_id": "org_...",
  "redwood.workspace_id": "ws_...",
  "redwood.deployment_mode": "hosted",

  "usr.id": "user_123",
  "usr.type": "user",
  "network.client.ip": "203.0.113.17",
  "http.useragent": "Mozilla/5.0...",

  "redwood.resource.type": "api_key",
  "redwood.resource.id": "key_456",

  "redwood.request.id": "req_...",
  "redwood.trace_id": "4bf92f...",
  "redwood.region": "us-east-1",

  "tags": [
    "vendor:redwood",
    "product:redwood-inference",
    "category:iam",
    "action:api_key.created",
    "outcome:success",
    "workspace:ws_...",
    "org:org_...",
    "region:us-east-1"
  ],

  "event": {"<full canonical event>": "..."}
}
```

### 10.2 Datadog facet recommendations
Promote these as facets for common queries:
- `redwood.audit.category`
- `redwood.audit.action`
- `redwood.audit.outcome`
- `redwood.org_id`
- `redwood.workspace_id`
- `usr.id` and `network.client.ip` (subject to privacy policy)
- `redwood.resource.type` and `redwood.resource.id`

Avoid promoting:
- `actor.display_name`
- `resource.display_name`
- `request.route` (unless customers explicitly want it; can explode cardinality)

---

## 11. Versioning + compatibility

### 11.1 Schema versioning policy
- `schema_version` MUST be present and parseable.
- Minor additions (new optional fields) may be introduced under `2.x` without breaking.
- Breaking changes require a major bump (e.g., `3.0`) and an export compatibility plan.

### 11.2 Action/category stability
- `category` and `action` are treated as stable contracts for:
  - customer filters
  - SIEM parsers
  - compliance evidence queries
- Renames are strongly discouraged. If required, we must:
  - emit both old and new action for a defined transition window OR
  - provide a documented alias mapping and update exporter-side transforms.

---

## 12. Checklist for adding a new audit event type
Before adding a new event type (new `action`):
1. Choose `category` from Section 5 and a stable `action` string.
2. Confirm required fields are present (Section 4.1).
3. Ensure no prohibited fields are included (Section 7.2).
4. If including `actor.ip` or `user_agent`, confirm collection is permitted and expected.
5. Add to:
   - category/action allowlist (filtering model)
   - SIEM mapping notes if a new common field is introduced
6. Request review from:
   - Security Engineering (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei)
   - Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park) if new tenant context fields are needed

---

## 13. Appendix: Reason codes (initial set)
Teams SHOULD use these `reason.code` values for failures/blocks when applicable:
- `permission_denied`
- `auth_failed`
- `validation_error`
- `not_found`
- `conflict`
- `rate_limited`
- `upstream_unavailable`
- `policy_blocked`

If adding a new reason code, update this appendix and ensure it is documented in customer-facing FAQ language where needed.

source.title: Evidence Pack v1: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement (Region Pinning) — Security & Compliance Foundation
source.author: Olga Petrov
source.body:
## 1. Purpose
This evidence pack provides auditor-ready artifacts for Redwood Inference’s **Hosted data residency enforcement** capability (“region pinning”). It is designed for:
- SOC 2 Type II evidence requests (Security, Availability, Confidentiality)
- ISO 27001 / ISO 27701 evidence requests where applicable
- Enterprise customer security questionnaires (data location, geo-fencing, auditability)

This pack focuses on **Hosted API** enforcement and notes alignment for **Dedicated** where behavior differs.

## 2. Control objective (plain language)
Redwood Inference ensures that when a customer configures an allowed set of regions for Hosted API (and a primary region), Redwood:
1) Evaluates residency policy deterministically at request time.
2) Routes and processes customer content only within the configured allowed region set.
3) Blocks requests that would violate policy using a consistent public error contract.
4) Records auditable events for residency policy configuration changes.
5) Provides monitoring and operational controls to detect and respond to enforcement issues (e.g., false positives).

## 3. Scope, system boundaries, and definitions
### 3.1 In-scope systems (Hosted)
- API Gateway (request validation, standardized error responses)
- Smart Routing layer (region selection + fallback constraints)
- Residency Policy Service (control plane configuration: org/project/key-level)
- Logging/telemetry related to residency decisions (decision context, enforcement reason codes)
- Audit Logging pipeline (create/update/delete policy events; admin overrides)
- Console / Admin UX (policy configuration and history view)

### 3.2 Out-of-scope / explicit exclusions (v1)
- Automatic multi-region replication of customer content
- Customer-defined geo-fencing rules for every internal subsystem (handled via phased hardening)
- Customer-managed encryption key residency requirements (KMS/HSM integrations covered under separate controls)

### 3.3 Definitions
- **Customer content:** request payloads (prompts/messages), uploaded files for inference where supported, and model outputs.
- **Residency policy:** configuration describing allowed region(s) and a primary region at org/project and optionally API key scope.
- **Allowed regions:** list/set of regions where customer content may be processed.
- **Cross-region fallback:** routing from primary region to another region due to capacity/health events.

## 4. Control mapping (SOC 2 / ISO crosswalk)
This section maps evidence to common auditor language. Exact SOC 2 criteria mappings may vary by audit firm.

### 4.1 Control statement DR-01 — Residency policy configuration is authorized and auditable
**Control intent:** Only authorized administrators can configure residency policies; all changes are logged.

**Key mechanisms**
- Residency Policy Service requires authenticated admin context for create/update/delete.
- Audit log events emitted for policy lifecycle changes (create/update/delete) and for admin overrides.
- Console provides configuration history for self-service review.

**Primary evidence**
- Audit log event samples (see Section 6.2)
- Console screenshots showing configuration history (see Section 6.3)
- Change management artifacts for enabling residency enforcement (see Section 7)

**Owners**
- Security Engineering: Marcus Lin
- GRC: Olga Petrov
- Platform: Ethan Park


### 4.2 Control statement DR-02 — Residency is enforced at request time with deterministic checks
**Control intent:** Requests are processed only in allowed regions; violations are blocked.

**Key mechanisms**
- API gateway validates residency policy context and returns standardized policy violation errors.
- Routing layer selects region from allowed set; cross-region fallback disabled by default unless explicitly allowed.
- Residency decision context is propagated downstream for observability and auditability.

**Primary evidence**
- ADRs documenting boundaries and error contract (see Section 5)
- End-to-end test results and CI gating artifacts (see Section 8)
- Blocked request metrics and dashboards (see Section 6.1)

**Owners**
- Routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Gateway: Amara Diallo
- SRE: Sean Gallagher


### 4.3 Control statement DR-03 — Security monitoring detects enforcement failures and abnormal block rates
**Control intent:** Redwood monitors enforcement health and investigates anomalies.

**Key mechanisms**
- Dashboards for blocked requests, evaluation latency, and cross-region routing attempts.
- Alerts for abnormal block-rate changes and policy evaluation failures.
- On-call runbook for false positives and rollback/feature-flag mitigation.

**Primary evidence**
- Monitoring spec + dashboard screenshots (see Section 6.1)
- Alert definitions and paging thresholds (see Section 6.1)
- Runbook and sample incident timeline (see Section 9)

**Owners**
- SRE: Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes
- Observability: Allison Grant


### 4.4 Control statement DR-04 — Customer-facing error responses are consistent and actionable
**Control intent:** Customers receive clear, machine-readable errors that facilitate remediation.

**Key mechanisms**
- Standardized HTTP status, error_code, request_id, and help_url.
- Same contract for streaming and non-streaming endpoints.
- SDK surfaces dedicated exception type for residency violations.

**Primary evidence**
- ADR-022 (error contract) and sample responses (Section 5)
- Gateway behavior validation tests (Section 8)
- Support tickets / fixes confirming streaming path correctness (Section 9.3)

**Owners**
- Product: Naomi Feldman
- Gateway: Brian Murphy / Amara Diallo


## 5. Design and policy documentation references (auditor context)
Provide these documents for narrative evidence and boundary clarity.

1) **Hosted Data Residency Policy and Controls (internal policy)**
- Location: `security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/hosted-data-residency-policy-and-controls`
- What it answers: what is guaranteed, what is excluded, how it maps to SOC2/ISO narratives.

2) **ADR-021: Region Pinning Enforcement Boundaries**
- Location: `architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-021-region-pinning-enforcement-boundaries`
- What it answers: definition of customer content; what subsystems are in-scope; explicit allowed cross-region metadata (non-content).

3) **ADR-022: Error Contract for Residency Violations**
- Location: `architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-022-error-contract-for-residency-violations`
- What it answers: HTTP status, error codes, message template requirements, request-id linkage, and SDK expectations.

4) **Residency Policy Service (system documentation)**
- Location: `eng-platform/systems-and-services/residency-policy-service`
- What it answers: schema, endpoints, auth model, validation, and integration points.


## 6. Operational evidence (monitoring, logs, audit events)
### 6.1 Monitoring dashboards and alerts
**Dashboard set name:** “Data Residency Enforcement”

**Required screenshots / exports (attach for audit package)**
- Dashboard overview (last 30 days):
  - `residency.blocked_requests.count` (by region, by org tier)
  - `residency.policy_eval.latency_ms.p95` (by region)
  - `routing.cross_region_attempts.count` (should be 0 unless explicitly allowed)
  - `gateway.residency_violations.count` (streaming vs non-streaming)
- Alert definitions page:
  - “Residency enforcement evaluation failures > 0 for 5m” (page)
  - “Blocked request rate delta > 3x baseline for 15m (per region)” (page)
  - “Cross-region attempt detected for pinned org” (page)

**Evidence notes for auditors**
- We provide dashboard screenshots for the audit period and can export raw metric time series upon request.
- For customer-sensitive data, we sanitize org identifiers in screenshots unless auditor has NDA and scoping includes customer names.


### 6.2 Audit log events (configuration changes)
**Event categories (expected)**
- `residency_policy.created`
- `residency_policy.updated`
- `residency_policy.deleted`
- `residency_policy.override_enabled` (admin-only, break-glass)
- `residency_policy.override_disabled`

**Fields auditors typically request**
- actor (user/service principal), actor_type
- org_id / project_id
- policy_id
- before/after summary (allowed_regions, primary_region)
- timestamp
- request_id / correlation_id

**Sample redacted event (format illustration)**
- event_type: `residency_policy.updated`
- timestamp: 2025-04-08T17:22:11Z
- actor_type: `user`
- actor: `admin@redwoodinference.com` (redacted in external exports)
- org_id: `org_8f3...`
- project_id: `proj_19c...`
- policy_id: `rp_2d1...`
- change_summary: `allowed_regions: [eu-central-1] -> [eu-central-1, eu-west-1]; primary_region: eu-central-1`
- correlation_id: `req_01HR...`

**How to produce evidence**
- Export from Audit Log Exporter: “Compliance Exports → Data Residency (Policy Changes)”
- Retention: per enterprise audit-log retention policy; minimum 1 year for enterprise tenants (see Security Logging & Monitoring policy).


### 6.3 Console/admin UX screenshots
Provide screenshots showing:
- Residency settings page (org-level and project-level)
- Allowed regions selector + primary region selector
- Confirmation modal and warning copy for restrictive policies
- Configuration history / “recent changes” view (timestamps and actor)
- “Blocked requests” troubleshooting view (shows error_code, request_id, block reason)

Redaction guidance:
- Mask customer org name if not in audit scope.
- Leave region names, error_code, and timestamps visible.


## 7. Change management artifacts (release governance)
Auditors frequently request proof that changes were reviewed, tested, and approved.

### 7.1 Architecture and security reviews
Provide:
- Threat model review summary (security sign-off). Include:
  - threats considered: cross-region content leakage via fallback, logging/tracing leakage, background tasks
  - mitigations and owners
  - acceptance notes for v1 exclusions
- Link to Security Engineering review thread (internal reference) and final approval date.

### 7.2 Implementation tracking and approvals
Provide:
- Launch checklist and rollout gates (staging → dogfood → allowlist customers → progressive rollout)
- List of feature flags used and who can toggle them
- Approvals:
  - Product sign-off (Naomi Feldman)
  - Security sign-off (Marcus Lin / CISO delegate)
  - SRE readiness sign-off (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes)

### 7.3 Code review evidence
Provide references to:
- Control plane schema + validation PR (reviewers and merge date)
- Routing enforcement PR (reviewers and merge date)
- Gateway error contract PR (reviewers and merge date)
- Audit event emission PR (reviewers and merge date)
- Observability pack PR (dashboard creation)

Evidence format:
- PR links with merge commit hashes and “Approved by” screenshots, if required by auditor.


## 8. Test evidence (design validation and operating effectiveness)
### 8.1 Test plan overview
**Test categories**
- Unit tests: residency policy evaluation logic, schema validation, precedence rules
- Integration tests: gateway-to-routing propagation of residency context
- E2E tests (CI gated): pinned EU org hitting US endpoint; streaming behavior; fallback scenarios
- Negative tests: invalid region IDs, empty allowed set, mismatched primary region

### 8.2 Required test artifacts
Attach/export for audit:
- Test suite execution report for audit period sample (e.g., one run per release)
- CI job logs showing pass/fail and commit SHA
- E2E test cases list with expected results

### 8.3 Example E2E test cases (for auditor readability)
1) **Pinned EU-only, request arrives at US edge**
- Given: allowed_regions = [EU], primary = EU
- When: request enters via US edge POP
- Then: request is routed to EU region; no cross-region fallback; logs show residency_selected_region in EU

2) **Pinned EU-only, EU capacity constrained**
- Given: allowed_regions = [EU], primary = EU
- When: EU region is degraded
- Then: request is blocked (no fallback outside allowed set)
- And: response matches standardized error contract

3) **Streaming endpoint residency violation**
- Given: allowed_regions excludes selected region
- When: streaming chat completion requested
- Then: initial stream frame returns standardized error structure and terminates stream cleanly


## 9. Operational readiness evidence (runbooks, incidents, support learnings)
### 9.1 Runbook: residency enforcement false positives
Provide:
- Runbook link and screenshots of key sections:
  - triage steps: confirm policy config, check routing attempt metrics, validate evaluation latency
  - mitigations: temporary allowlist expansion, feature-flag override (break-glass), rollback
  - customer comms template guidance

### 9.2 Incident evidence (if applicable in audit period)
If a relevant incident occurred during the audit window, provide:
- incident ticket ID
- timeline summary
- customer impact summary
- corrective actions and verification steps

### 9.3 Support case evidence (quality and consistency of enforcement)
Provide at least one redacted support ticket showing:
- the problem (e.g., confusing error or unexpected fallback attempt)
- investigation notes referencing residency decision context
- resolution (bug fix or config guidance)


## 10. Evidence index (what to attach / provide on request)
Use this as a checklist when assembling the final audit packet.

### 10.1 Governance / policy
- Hosted Data Residency Policy and Controls (internal policy doc)
- ADR-021 (enforcement boundaries)
- ADR-022 (error contract)

### 10.2 Technical controls (system and code)
- Residency Policy Service system doc
- PR references (schema/validation, routing enforcement, gateway errors, audit events)
- Configuration validation rules documentation (allowed_regions, primary_region constraints)

### 10.3 Monitoring and detection
- Dashboard screenshots (30-day view, by region)
- Alert definitions and recent alert history (redacted if needed)
- Sample queries used to detect cross-region routing attempts

### 10.4 Auditability
- Audit log export samples for policy create/update/delete
- Evidence of retention configuration for audit logs

### 10.5 Testing
- CI job logs for E2E suite (commit SHA + timestamp)
- Test case list and results summary
- Evidence that streaming endpoints return standardized residency errors

### 10.6 Operational readiness
- Runbook(s)
- On-call training note or readiness sign-off (if maintained)
- Incident postmortem(s), if any within audit scope


## 11. Redaction and data handling for audit submissions
- Default: redact customer names, org IDs, and API keys.
- Leave: region names, error_code, policy logic descriptions, timestamps, and aggregated metrics.
- For auditors under NDA requesting raw logs: provide time-bounded exports with minimized fields and approval from Security (Marcus Lin) and GRC (Olga Petrov).


## 12. Point of contact and evidence production workflow
**Primary GRC contact:** Olga Petrov (evidence coordination, auditor Q&A)
**Security Engineering:** Marcus Lin (technical control narrative, threat model)
**SRE:** Sean Gallagher (monitoring/alerts, operational effectiveness)
**Product:** Naomi Feldman (customer-facing scope, error messaging commitments)

**Standard turnaround targets**
- Initial evidence packet: 3–5 business days
- Follow-up samples/exports: 5–10 business days (depending on redaction and approvals)


## 13. Appendix: Standardized residency violation error (public contract excerpt)
(Include as a readability aid; authoritative source is ADR-022.)

- HTTP status: 409 (Policy conflict) for residency violations
- error_code: `RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION`
- Required fields: `message`, `request_id`, `help_url`
- Optional fields (subject to final policy): `policy_scope` (org/project/key), `allowed_regions`, `selected_region`

Example (redacted):
- status: 409
- error_code: RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION
- message: "Request blocked by data residency policy. Configure an allowed region that includes the target region, or send requests to your pinned primary region."
- request_id: req_01HRXXXX
- help_url: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/security/data-residency#region-pinning-troubleshooting
