source.title: Legal hold, incident-related holds, and retention exceptions policy
source.author: Sofia Mendes
source.body:
## 1. Purpose
This policy defines when and how Redwood Inference applies (a) **legal holds**, (b) **incident-related holds**, and (c) **retention exceptions** to standard data retention behavior. It also specifies the required **approvals**, **documentation**, and **audit trail** for any hold or exception.

This document is intended to be customer-ready in concept (what we do and why), but contains internal process and tooling references. Share externally only after Security & Legal review.

## 2. Scope
This policy applies to all Redwood Inference product tiers (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**) and all Redwood-managed environments. For Private deployments with customer-managed infrastructure, the same controls apply where supported; some enforcement steps may be performed by the customer with Redwood guidance.

### 2.1 In-scope data classes (retention-controlled)
Retention controls and exceptions may apply to these data classes (as implemented for the Retention Controls + Compliance Reporting Pack project):
- **Request/response logs** (API gateway and serving runtime request metadata; may include redacted payload fields depending on logging mode)
- **Prompts/completions where stored** (only when a customer has explicitly enabled storage for debugging/evals; not all tiers store this)
- **Audit logs** (control plane events including policy changes, user/admin actions, and security-sensitive operations)
- **Traces/metrics** (observability telemetry; typically aggregated)
- **Artifacts** (where applicable): evaluation datasets, report-pack bundles, and configuration exports

### 2.2 Out of scope
- Customer primary content stored in customer systems (e.g., prompts stored by customer applications)
- Any “per-field” redaction policies (handled under logging standards)

## 3. Definitions
- **Retention policy**: A tenant- and data-class-scoped configuration defining TTL, purge semantics, and optional exceptions.
- **Hold**: A temporary override that prevents deletion of data that would otherwise be purged under TTL.
- **Legal hold**: A hold initiated for litigation, regulatory inquiry, preservation request, or similar legal obligation.
- **Incident-related hold**: A hold initiated to support incident response, forensics, or security investigation (including suspected compromise).
- **Exception**: A deviation from standard retention defaults (shorter retention, longer retention, temporary suspension of purge, or temporary enabling of additional logging) that is approved and time-bounded.
- **Break-glass**: Emergency access or configuration changes used to restore service or investigate high-severity issues, with post-action review and auditing.

## 4. Policy statement (requirements)
1) Holds and exceptions **must be time-bounded** and tied to an authoritative tracking item (Jira/Linear case, legal matter ID, or incident ID).
2) Holds and exceptions **must be approved** according to the matrix in Section 7.
3) Holds and exceptions **must be auditable**: request, approval, implementation, and release must generate audit log events and be exportable.
4) Holds and exceptions **must be minimally scoped**:
   - smallest feasible tenant(s)
   - smallest feasible data class(es)
   - smallest feasible region(s)
   - shortest feasible time window
5) Holds and exceptions **must not silently change customer-facing behavior**. If customer-visible impacts exist (e.g., extended retention of request logs), Customer Success must be informed and customer notification must follow contract and security guidance.

## 5. Legal hold policy
### 5.1 When a legal hold may be applied
A legal hold may be applied when Redwood Legal determines we have a preservation obligation, including:
- litigation hold notices
- subpoenas / regulatory inquiries
- customer contractual preservation requests routed through Legal
- internal investigations requiring evidence preservation under counsel direction

### 5.2 Who can request a legal hold
- Legal team (primary)
- CISO or Security Engineering (may request Legal evaluation)
- GRC (may request Legal evaluation for audit/regulatory inquiries)

### 5.3 Required documentation
A legal hold request must include:
- legal matter reference (internal Legal matter ID or equivalent)
- scope: tenant(s), environment(s), region(s), data class(es)
- reason for hold and preservation requirements
- expected duration or review cadence
- customer communication plan (if applicable)

### 5.4 Implementation requirements
- Legal holds are implemented as a **Hold Override** in the Retention Policy Service (RPS) and must:
  - explicitly list affected tenant(s)
  - explicitly list affected data class(es)
  - specify an effective start timestamp
  - specify review date (required) and an expiration timestamp (recommended; if omitted, requires explicit renewal and quarterly review)
- Legal holds must prevent TTL purge jobs from deleting data within scope, including backfill deletes.
- Legal holds must not disable ingestion. They are “preserve what exists/arrives” holds unless Legal specifies otherwise.
- For Private deployments: Redwood provides signed instructions and validates evidence of hold configuration when Redwood is contractually responsible for compliance reporting.

### 5.5 Review, renewal, and release
- Legal holds require **quarterly review** by Legal (or sooner if specified).
- Release (removal) requires Legal approval and must be recorded in the audit log with the release rationale.
- Upon release, the system returns to the configured retention policy. Data accumulated during the hold becomes eligible for purge according to TTL, but purge must proceed in a controlled manner (see Section 9.4 on safe purge operations).

## 6. Incident-related hold policy
### 6.1 When an incident-related hold may be applied
An incident-related hold may be applied for:
- SEV-1/SEV-2 incidents requiring extended debugging beyond normal retention
- confirmed or suspected security incidents (including credential misuse, data exfiltration concerns, or integrity issues)
- forensics or root cause analysis requiring preservation of logs, traces, or audit evidence

### 6.2 Default duration and constraints
- Default duration: **7 days**
- Maximum duration without CISO approval: **30 days**
- Must be scoped to the minimal set of tenants and data classes necessary.

### 6.3 Authority and process
- The on-call Incident Commander (IC) or Security Incident Lead may request an incident-related hold.
- Implementation must be performed by Platform on-call or a designated Retention Controls engineer.
- A post-incident review must include:
  - confirmation of whether the hold is still required
  - a planned release date
  - impact assessment (storage growth, privacy implications)

### 6.4 Break-glass usage
Break-glass configuration changes may be used to apply a hold quickly in a high-severity incident, but require:
- creation of an incident ticket before or immediately after action (within 2 hours)
- explicit “break-glass” flag in the audit event (see Section 8)
- Security review within 1 business day

## 7. Retention exception workflow (non-hold)
Exceptions are deviations from standard retention defaults. Common examples:
- Shortening retention for request logs to meet customer policy
- Temporarily enabling additional logging for debugging (with explicit customer consent where required)
- Temporarily pausing purge jobs for a specific store due to integrity concerns (e.g., suspected deletion bug)

### 7.1 Exception request requirements
All exception requests must include:
- tenant/org identifier(s)
- affected data class(es)
- requested change (e.g., TTL from 30d to 7d)
- start time and explicit end time
- reason and risk assessment (privacy, operational, compliance)
- verification plan (how we confirm desired behavior)

### 7.2 Standard workflow (non-emergency)
1) Requestor opens a ticket (Linear for engineering-driven, Jira for support/customer-driven) and tags **Security & Compliance**.
2) Security Engineering reviews for safety and audit requirements.
3) Legal and/or GRC review if the exception affects contractual retention, regulatory obligations, or customer commitments.
4) Implementer applies the change via the Retention Policy Service (or Private deployment mechanism) with correct audit annotations.
5) SRE confirms verification metrics within 24 hours.
6) Exception is automatically reverted at end time (where supported), otherwise manual revert is scheduled and tracked.

### 7.3 Emergency workflow
For production safety or customer impact mitigation:
- SRE/Platform may take immediate action to pause purge for a store or adjust throttles.
- The action must be logged as “emergency exception” and reviewed by Security Engineering within 1 business day.
- Any emergency exception longer than 72 hours requires CISO (or delegate) approval.

## 8. Approval matrix
Approval requirements apply to both initiating and releasing holds/exceptions, unless explicitly noted.

**Legend:**
- Required approver(s) must explicitly approve in the tracking ticket.
- “Security Eng” refers to the Security Engineering on-call or designated reviewer.

Approval matrix (minimum):
- Apply Legal Hold: Required approvals = Legal (Sofia Mendes or delegate) + CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman or delegate). Notify = GRC (Olga Petrov), Platform/SRE.
- Release Legal Hold: Required approvals = Legal + CISO. Notify = GRC.
- Apply Incident-Related Hold (<= 7 days): Required approvals = Incident Commander + Security Eng. Notify = SRE lead.
- Extend Incident-Related Hold (8–30 days): Required approvals = Security Eng + SRE lead (Sean Gallagher or delegate).
- Extend Incident-Related Hold (> 30 days): Required approvals = CISO + Legal (if any legal/regulatory considerations).
- Retention Exception (shorten TTL): Required approvals = Security Eng + Product (Naomi Feldman or delegate) if customer-facing behavior changes; Legal/GRC if contractual.
- Retention Exception (lengthen TTL): Required approvals = Security Eng + GRC; Legal if customer contract/regulatory.
- Pause Purge Jobs (store-wide, any tenant): Required approvals = SRE lead + Security Eng; CISO if > 72 hours.
- Enable Additional Logging for Debugging (may increase stored sensitive data): Required approvals = Security Eng + Legal (if customer data impact) + Customer consent documented by CS/SE.

If an approver is unavailable and time-sensitive action is required, follow break-glass rules in Section 6.4 and document the escalation path.

## 9. Audit trail and evidence requirements
### 9.1 Audit logging is mandatory
Every hold/exception action must generate an **audit log event** in the control plane.

At minimum, events must capture:
- event type (e.g., retention.hold.applied, retention.hold.released, retention.exception.approved, retention.exception.applied)
- requester identity (human user or service account), auth method, and source IP (where available)
- approver identity(ies)
- tenant(s)/org(s) affected
- data class(es) affected
- region(s)/environment(s) affected
- effective timestamp(s) and expiration/review date
- reason string (human-readable) and ticket reference(s)
- change payload hash (SHA-256 over normalized JSON of the hold/exception config)
- break-glass flag (true/false)

### 9.2 Where audit evidence is stored and retained
- Audit logs are stored in the audit log store and are exportable via the audit-log exporter.
- Audit logs have their own retention policy and are not shortened by customer request unless approved by Security & Compliance and Legal.
- Compliance pack generation must be able to bundle:
  - retention configuration export
  - hold/exception change history for the requested window
  - audit log export matching report template filters

### 9.3 Required linkage to tickets and artifacts
All holds/exceptions must link to at least one authoritative record:
- Incident-related: incident ID + postmortem link (when complete)
- Legal hold: legal matter ID
- Customer-driven exceptions: customer support ticket ID + customer approval evidence (email/contract addendum) if applicable

### 9.4 Operational safety requirements (implementation)
When holds are released or TTLs are shortened, purge backlogs can spike. Implementers must:
- use idempotent purge jobs with rate limits
- monitor TTL lag and purge error metrics during the first 24 hours
- coordinate with SRE if projected delete volume exceeds store safety thresholds

## 10. Customer communication guidelines
- Legal holds are generally not disclosed to customers unless required by law or contract.
- Incident-related holds and retention exceptions that materially change customer data handling must be communicated to the customer when:
  - the customer requested the change, or
  - contract/SOC2/ISO reporting expectations require disclosure, or
  - the change affects data residency, retention, or privacy commitments.

All external communication about holds/exceptions must be reviewed by Customer Success + Security, and by Legal when contractual language is involved.

## 11. Roles and responsibilities
- Legal (Sofia Mendes): legal hold decisions, quarterly reviews, approval for release.
- CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman): final security sign-off for legal holds and long-running incident holds.
- Security Engineering (Marcus Lin or delegate): reviews for security/privacy impact; verifies audit trail quality.
- GRC (Olga Petrov): ensures evidence suitability; maintains checklist for audits/customer requests.
- SRE (Sean Gallagher): operational safety, purge throttling, and monitoring during changes.
- Product (Naomi Feldman): customer-facing behavior alignment and documentation requirements.

## 12. Compliance alignment (informative)
This policy supports common control expectations (SOC 2 / ISO-style) around:
- change management and approvals for security-relevant configuration
- logging and monitoring of administrative actions
- retention, deletion, and preservation controls
- evidence generation and auditability

## 13. Exceptions to this policy
Any exception to this policy (e.g., applying a hold without audit logging, or without required approvals) is itself a security exception and must be:
- documented in a Security exception ticket
- approved by the CISO
- remediated with a defined timeline

## 14. Revision history
- 2025-02-12: Initial published version aligned to Retention Controls + Compliance Reporting Pack Phase 1.
- 2025-03-08: Clarified approval thresholds for incident hold extensions and emergency purge pauses; expanded audit trail fields to include payload hash and break-glass flag.

title: Legal hold, incident-related holds, and retention exceptions policy
preview:
## 1. Purpose
This policy defines when and how Redwood Inference applies (a) **legal holds**, (b) **incident-related holds**, and (c) **retention exceptions** to standard data retention behavior. It also specifies the required **approvals**, **documentation**, and **audit trail** for any hold or exception.

This document is intended to be customer-ready in concept (what we do and why), but contains internal process and tooling references. Share externally only after Security & Legal review.

## 2. Scope
This policy applies to all Redwood Inference product tiers (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**) and all Redwood-managed environments. For Private deployments with customer-managed infrastructure, the same controls apply where supported; some enforcement steps may be performed by the customer with Redwood guidance.

### 2.1 In-scope data classes (retention-controlled)
Retention controls and exceptions may apply to these data classes (as implemented for the Retention Controls + Compliance Reporting Pack project):
- **Request/response logs** (API gateway and serving runtime request metadata; may include redacted payload fields depending on logging mode)
- **Prompts/completions where stored** (only when a customer has explicitly enabled storage for debugging/evals; not all tiers store this)
- **Audit logs** (control plane events including policy changes, user/admin actions, and security-sensitive operations)
- **Traces/metrics** (observability telemetry; typically aggregated)
- **Artifacts** (where applicable): evaluation datasets, report-pack bundles, and configuration exports

### 2.2 Out of scope
- Customer primary content stored in customer systems (e.g., prompts stored by customer applications)
- Any “per-field” redaction policies (handled under logging standards)

## 3. Definitions
- **Retention policy**: A tenant- and data-class-scoped configuration defining TTL, purge semantics, and optional exceptions.
- **Hold**: A temporary override that prevents deletion of data that would otherwise be purged under TTL.
- **Legal hold**: A hold initiated for litigation, regulatory inquiry, preservation request, or similar legal obligation.
- **Incident-related hold**: A hold initiated to support incident response, forensics, or security investigation (including suspected compromise).
- **Exception**: A deviation from standard retention defaults (shorter retention, longer retention, temporary suspension of purge, or temporary enabling of additional logging) that is approved and time-bounded.
- **Break-glass**: Emergency access or configuration changes used to restore service or investigate high-severity issues, with post-action review and auditing.

## 4. Policy statement (requirements)
1) Holds and exceptions **must be time-bounded** and tied to an authoritative tracking item (Jira/Linear case, legal matter ID, or incident ID).
2) Holds and exceptions **must be approved** according to the matrix in Section 7.
3) Holds and exceptions **must be auditable**: request, approval, implementation, and release must generate audit log events and be exportable.
4) Holds and exceptions **must be minimally scoped**:
   - smallest feasible tenant(s)
   - smallest feasible data class(es)
   - smallest feasible region(s)
   - shortest feasible time window
5) Holds and exceptions **must not silently change customer-facing behavior**. If customer-visible impacts exist (e.g., extended retention of request logs), Customer Success must be informed and customer notification must follow contract and security guidance.

## 5. Legal hold policy
### 5.1 When a legal hold may be applied
A legal hold may be applied when Redwood Legal determines we have a preservation obligation, including:
- litigation hold notices
- subpoenas / regulatory inquiries
- customer contractual preservation requests routed through Legal
- internal investigations requiring evidence preservation under counsel direction

### 5.2 Who can request a legal hold
- Legal team (primary)
- CISO or Security Engineering (may request Legal evaluation)
- GRC (may request Legal evaluation for audit/regulatory inquiries)

### 5.3 Required documentation
A legal hold request must include:
- legal matter reference (internal Legal matter ID or equivalent)
- scope: tenant(s), environment(s), region(s), data class(es)
- reason for hold and preservation requirements
- expected duration or review cadence
- customer communication plan (if applicable)

### 5.4 Implementation requirements
- Legal holds are implemented as a **Hold Override** in the Retention Policy Service (RPS) and must:
  - explicitly list affected tenant(s)
  - explicitly list affected data class(es)
  - specify an effective start timestamp
  - specify review date (required) and an expiration timestamp (recommended; if omitted, requires explicit renewal and quarterly review)
- Legal holds must prevent TTL purge jobs from deleting data within scope, including backfill deletes.
- Legal holds must not disable ingestion. They are “preserve what exists/arrives” holds unless Legal specifies otherwise.
- For Private deployments: Redwood provides signed instructions and validates evidence of hold configuration when Redwood is contractually responsible for compliance reporting.

### 5.5 Review, renewal, and release
- Legal holds require **quarterly review** by Legal (or sooner if specified).
- Release (removal) requires Legal approval and must be recorded in the audit log with the release rationale.
- Upon release, the system returns to the configured retention policy. Data accumulated during the hold becomes eligible for purge according to TTL, but purge must proceed in a controlled manner (see Section 9.4 on safe purge operations).

## 6. Incident-related hold policy
### 6.1 When an incident-related hold may be applied
An incident-related hold may be applied for:
- SEV-1/SEV-2 incidents requiring extended debugging beyond normal retention
- confirmed or suspected security incidents (including credential misuse, data exfiltration concerns, or integrity issues)
- forensics or root cause analysis requiring preservation of logs, traces, or audit evidence

### 6.2 Default duration and constraints
- Default duration: **7 days**
- Maximum duration without CISO approval: **30 days**
- Must be scoped to the minimal set of tenants and data classes necessary.

### 6.3 Authority and process
- The on-call Incident Commander (IC) or Security Incident Lead may request an incident-related hold.
- Implementation must be performed by Platform on-call or a designated Retention Controls engineer.
- A post-incident review must include:
  - confirmation of whether the hold is still required
  - a planned release date
  - impact assessment (storage growth, privacy implications)

### 6.4 Break-glass usage
Break-glass configuration changes may be used to apply a hold quickly in a high-severity incident, but require:
- creation of an incident ticket before or immediately after action (within 2 hours)
- explicit “break-glass” flag in the audit event (see Section 8)
- Security review within 1 business day

## 7. Retention exception workflow (non-hold)
Exceptions are deviations from standard retention defaults. Common examples:
- Shortening retention for request logs to meet customer policy
- Temporarily enabling additional logging for debugging (with explicit customer consent where required)
- Temporarily pausing purge jobs for a specific store due to integrity concerns (e.g., suspected deletion bug)

### 7.1 Exception request requirements
All exception requests must include:
- tenant/org identifier(s)
- affected data class(es)
- requested change (e.g., TTL from 30d to 7d)
- start time and explicit end time
- reason and risk assessment (privacy, operational, compliance)
- verification plan (how we confirm desired behavior)

### 7.2 Standard workflow (non-emergency)
1) Requestor opens a ticket (Linear for engineering-driven, Jira for support/customer-driven) and tags **Security & Compliance**.
2) Security Engineering reviews for safety and audit requirements.
3) Legal and/or GRC review if the exception affects contractual retention, regulatory obligations, or customer commitments.
4) Implementer applies the change via the Retention Policy Service (or Private deployment mechanism) with correct audit annotations.
5) SRE confirms verification metrics within 24 hours.
6) Exception is automatically reverted at end time (where supported), otherwise manual revert is scheduled and tracked.

### 7.3 Emergency workflow
For production safety or customer impact mitigation:
- SRE/Platform may take immediate action to pause purge for a store or adjust throttles.
- The action must be logged as “emergency exception” and reviewed by Security Engineering within 1 business day.
- Any emergency exception longer than 72 hours requires CISO (or delegate) approval.

## 8. Approval matrix
Approval requirements apply to both initiating and releasing holds/exceptions, unless explicitly noted.

**Legend:**
- Required approver(s) must explicitly approve in the tracking ticket.
- “Security Eng” refers to the Security Engineering on-call or designated reviewer.

Approval matrix (minimum):
- Apply Legal Hold: Required approvals = Legal (Sofia Mendes or delegate) + CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman or delegate). Notify = GRC (Olga Petrov), Platform/SRE.
- Release Legal Hold: Required approvals = Legal + CISO. Notify = GRC.
- Apply Incident-Related Hold (<= 7 days): Required approvals = Incident Commander + Security Eng. Notify = SRE lead.
- Extend Incident-Related Hold (8–30 days): Required approvals = Security Eng + SRE lead (Sean Gallagher or delegate).
- Extend Incident-Related Hold (> 30 days): Required approvals = CISO + Legal (if any legal/regulatory considerations).
- Retention Exception (shorten TTL): Required approvals = Security Eng + Product (Naomi Feldman or delegate) if customer-facing behavior changes; Legal/GRC if contractual.
- Retention Exception (lengthen TTL): Required approvals = Security Eng + GRC; Legal if customer contract/regulatory.
- Pause Purge Jobs (store-wide, any tenant): Required approvals = SRE lead + Security Eng; CISO if > 72 hours.
- Enable Additional Logging for Debugging (may increase stored sensitive data): Required approvals = Security Eng + Legal (if customer data impact) + Customer consent documented by CS/SE.

If an approver is unavailable and time-sensitive action is required, follow break-glass rules in Section 6.4 and document the escalation path.

## 9. Audit trail and evidence requirements
### 9.1 Audit logging is mandatory
Every hold/exception action must generate an **audit log event** in the control plane.

At minimum, events must capture:
- event type (e.g., retention.hold.applied, retention.hold.released, retention.exception.approved, retention.exception.applied)
- requester identity (human user or service account), auth method, and source IP (where available)
- approver identity(ies)
- tenant(s)/org(s) affected
- data class(es) affected
- region(s)/environment(s) affected
- effective timestamp(s) and expiration/review date
- reason string (human-readable) and ticket reference(s)
- change payload hash (SHA-256 over normalized JSON of the hold/exception config)
- break-glass flag (true/false)

### 9.2 Where audit evidence is stored and retained
- Audit logs are stored in the audit log store and are exportable via the audit-log exporter.
- Audit logs have their own retention policy and are not shortened by customer request unless approved by Security & Compliance and Legal.
- Compliance pack generation must be able to bundle:
  - retention configuration export
  - hold/exception change history for the requested window
  - audit log export matching report template filters

### 9.3 Required linkage to tickets and artifacts
All holds/exceptions must link to at least one authoritative record:
- Incident-related: incident ID + postmortem link (when complete)
- Legal hold: legal matter ID
- Customer-driven exceptions: customer support ticket ID + customer approval evidence (email/contract addendum) if applicable

### 9.4 Operational safety requirements (implementation)
When holds are released or TTLs are shortened, purge backlogs can spike. Implementers must:
- use idempotent purge jobs with rate limits
- monitor TTL lag and purge error metrics during the first 24 hours
- coordinate with SRE if projected delete volume exceeds store safety thresholds

## 10. Customer communication guidelines
- Legal holds are generally not disclosed to customers unless required by law or contract.
- Incident-related holds and retention exceptions that materially change customer data handling must be communicated to the customer when:
  - the customer requested the change, or
  - contract/SOC2/ISO reporting expectations require disclosure, or
  - the change affects data residency, retention, or privacy commitments.

All external communication about holds/exceptions must be reviewed by Customer Success + Security, and by Legal when contractual language is involved.

## 11. Roles and responsibilities
- Legal (Sofia Mendes): legal hold decisions, quarterly reviews, approval for release.
- CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman): final security sign-off for legal holds and long-running incident holds.
- Security Engineering (Marcus Lin or delegate): reviews for security/privacy impact; verifies audit trail quality.
- GRC (Olga Petrov): ensures evidence suitability; maintains checklist for audits/customer requests.
- SRE (Sean Gallagher): operational safety, purge throttling, and monitoring during changes.
- Product (Naomi Feldman): customer-facing behavior alignment and documentation requirements.

## 12. Compliance alignment (informative)
This policy supports common control expectations (SOC 2 / ISO-style) around:
- change management and approvals for security-relevant configuration
- logging and monitoring of administrative actions
- retention, deletion, and preservation controls
- evidence generation and auditability

## 13. Exceptions to this policy
Any exception to this policy (e.g., applying a hold without audit logging, or without required approvals) is itself a security exception and must be:
- documented in a Security exception ticket
- approved by the CISO
- remediated with a defined timeline

## 14. Revision history
- 2025-02-12: Initial published version aligned to Retention Controls + Compliance Reporting Pack Phase 1.
- 2025-03-08: Clarified approval thresholds for incident hold extensions and emergency purge pauses; expanded audit trail fields to include payload hash and break-glass flag.

source.body:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s internal policy for **Hosted API data residency** and the required **engineering and operational controls** used to enforce it. It is written to:
- Provide a clear, auditable statement of what residency guarantees are provided for Hosted API.
- Define enforcement boundaries (what counts as “customer content” and what must remain in-region).
- Specify required controls across routing, fallback behavior, logging/telemetry, and background processing.
- Map controls to common compliance frameworks (SOC 2 and ISO/IEC 27001) for audit narratives.

This policy supports the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative: **Hosted data residency enforcement (region pinning)**.

## 2. Scope
### 2.1 In scope
- **Redwood Hosted API** request handling for text generation/chat, embeddings, reranking, and streaming/non-streaming variants.
- Request routing and model selection (**Smart Routing**) and related fallback behavior.
- API request validation and error responses (**API Gateway**).
- Logging and telemetry systems that may contain customer content (application logs, traces, debug payloads).
- Background processing directly triggered by Hosted API requests (e.g., asynchronous request completion, retry workers, moderation/classification where applicable).
- Administrative configuration for residency policies (**Residency Policy Service** in the control plane).

### 2.2 Out of scope (initial release)
- Automatic multi-region replication of customer data.
- Advanced geo-fencing beyond request routing and logging boundaries (handled via phased hardening).
- Customer-managed encryption key residency guarantees (covered separately under key management policy).
- Dedicated and Private deployments (covered under separate service-specific residency statements; Dedicated may implement aligned region selection but is not governed by Hosted control plane policy objects unless explicitly configured).

## 3. Definitions
### 3.1 Residency terms
- **Region**: A public cloud region (e.g., eu-west-1) mapped to a Redwood internal region identifier (e.g., EU1). Region mapping is maintained by Platform Engineering (see internal region mapping sheet).
- **Allowed regions**: The set of regions in which customer content is permitted to be processed for an org/project/API key.
- **Primary region**: The default region for routing when multiple regions are allowed.
- **Region pinning**: A configuration that restricts Hosted API processing to an allowed region set.

### 3.2 Customer content (enforcement boundary)
For this policy, **customer content** includes:
- Request prompts/messages, tool/function payloads, and attachments that are provided to the model.
- Model outputs (tokens, completions, embeddings vectors derived from customer-provided input).
- Customer-provided metadata that can contain sensitive data (e.g., “user_message”, “context”, “documents”).
- Any derived artifacts that include reconstructable content (e.g., cached prefixes/KV cache entries, debug dumps).

Customer content does **not** include:
- Non-content operational metadata that does not contain sensitive data (e.g., request_id, org_id, project_id, model_id, region_id, error_code, timestamps, latency metrics).

Note: These boundaries must match ADR-021 “Region Pinning Enforcement Boundaries”. Where ambiguity exists, classify as customer content.

## 4. Policy statement (what we guarantee)
### 4.1 Hosted residency guarantee
If an organization/project/API key is configured with an allowed region set, Redwood guarantees that:
1) **Processing location**: Customer content for Hosted API requests will be processed only in the allowed region(s).
2) **No cross-region fallback with content**: Routing and fallback behavior will not send customer content to a region outside the allowed set.
3) **Deterministic enforcement**: Requests that would violate policy are blocked at request time (or at the earliest deterministic point for streaming) with a consistent error contract.
4) **Logging/telemetry boundaries**: Systems that could store customer content (including traces and debug logs) will store such content only in the allowed region(s) or will be content-redacted before leaving the region.
5) **Administrative auditability**: Changes to residency configuration are recorded in audit logs with actor, timestamp, and change details.

### 4.2 Supported residency modes
- **Single-region pin**: Allowed regions contains exactly one region (e.g., EU-only).
- **Multi-region allowed set**: Allowed regions contains multiple regions (e.g., EU + UK) with a primary.
- **No pinning**: Default routing behavior applies; residency guarantee does not apply.

### 4.3 Precedence rules (which policy applies)
Policy precedence is:
1) API key-level policy (most specific)
2) Project-level policy
3) Org-level policy (least specific)
4) No residency policy

If conflicting policies exist, the most specific applicable policy is used.

## 5. Explicit exclusions / limitations
The following are excluded from the Hosted residency guarantee unless contractually stated otherwise and implemented under separate controls:
- **Client-side routing**: If customers send requests to a region-specific endpoint that is not in their allowed set, the request will be blocked; Redwood does not control customer network paths.
- **Customer-managed storage**: Content that customers export/download/store in their own systems is outside Redwood’s residency scope.
- **Third-party processors outside region**: If a customer enables optional integrations that transmit content externally (e.g., webhooks to their own endpoints), residency depends on customer configuration.
- **Support workflows**: Support will not request raw customer prompts/outputs for troubleshooting unless explicitly approved by the customer and handled via approved secure channels; such handling is governed by Support Data Handling Policy.
- **Aggregated metrics**: Non-content operational metrics may be stored in centralized systems outside the pinned region (see Section 7.4). Metrics must not include customer content.
- **Model provider constraints**: For hosted open models running on Redwood-managed infrastructure, processing occurs in-region; if any future offering involves third-party model endpoints, it must be explicitly excluded or separately controlled and disclosed.

## 6. Control objectives
Controls must meet these objectives:
- **CO-1 (Policy correctness)**: The evaluated policy matches configured intent and is consistent across gateway and routing.
- **CO-2 (Enforcement)**: Any routing decision that would move customer content out of allowed regions is blocked.
- **CO-3 (Observability)**: Residency decisions and blocks are observable without leaking customer content.
- **CO-4 (Auditability)**: Config changes are logged and retrievable for auditors and incident response.
- **CO-5 (Operational safety)**: Enforcement failures default to a safe posture (block or in-region), with clear incident procedures.

## 7. Required engineering controls
### 7.1 Control plane: Residency Policy Service
**Requirement:** Residency policy objects must be stored and served by the control plane with authorization and validation.

Minimum requirements:
- AuthZ: Only authorized org/project admins (and internal admin roles) can create/update/delete policies.
- Validation:
  - allowed_regions must be a non-empty list when policy is enabled.
  - primary_region must be a member of allowed_regions.
  - Regions must be valid and currently supported for Hosted.
- Change control:
  - Mutations must emit an **audit log event**.
  - Policy versions must be retrievable (history) for internal investigations.

### 7.2 API Gateway: request-time validation & error contract
**Requirement:** The gateway must validate residency policy before forwarding request content to downstream systems.

- The gateway fetches or is provided residency policy context (policy_id, allowed_regions, primary_region, enforcement_mode).
- If the incoming request is targeted to or would be processed in a disallowed region, the gateway must block.
- Errors must follow the public error contract (ADR-022):
  - HTTP status code: **409 Conflict** (policy conflict) for residency violations.
  - Machine-readable error code (example): `residency_policy_violation`.
  - Response includes `request_id` and a stable `help_url`.
  - Response must not include customer content.

Streaming: If streaming starts, the system must not stream any model output if the request later evaluates as disallowed. Residency evaluation must be performed before tokens are generated.

### 7.3 Smart Routing: region selection and fallback constraints
**Requirement:** Routing decisions must treat residency as a hard constraint.

Rules:
- Candidate regions = allowed_regions (if policy exists) else default routing regions.
- Primary region is selected when healthy and capacity is available.
- Failover/fallback is permitted **only** within allowed_regions.
- Cross-region fallback outside allowed_regions is prohibited unless an explicit, customer-configured exception exists (see Section 10).

Routing must emit a structured **residency decision context** into logs/telemetry:
- request_id, org_id, project_id
- evaluated_policy_id (if any)
- allowed_regions
- selected_region
- decision_outcome: allow | block
- block_reason (if block)

### 7.4 Logging, telemetry, and tracing
**Requirement:** Customer content must not leave the allowed region via logs/traces.

Controls:
- Content redaction: Default logging configuration must redact customer content fields. Any “debug logging” that includes content must be region-scoped storage and gated behind time-limited feature flags with Security approval.
- Trace payloads: Traces may include identifiers and timing but must not include prompt text, generated text, or embedding vectors.
- Central metrics: Aggregated counters/histograms may be centrally stored cross-region if they contain no customer content (e.g., counts by error_code, latency percentiles, block rate).
- Residency decision logs: Must not include raw prompts/outputs; only structured metadata.

### 7.5 Caching and intermediate artifacts
**Requirement:** Any caching that may contain customer content (prefix/KV caches, request replay buffers) must be region-local.

- Cache keys and values must not replicate across regions.
- Cache warming strategies must not copy customer content cross-region.
- For shared multi-tenant caches, tenant isolation controls still apply; residency adds region isolation.

### 7.6 Background processing and retries
**Requirement:** Background tasks triggered by Hosted requests must execute in-region.

- Retry workers must be region-affine.
- Queueing systems must not move message payloads (containing content) outside region.
- Any exception must be treated as a residency incident.

### 7.7 Security testing and release gates
**Requirement:** Residency enforcement must be validated with automated and manual tests.

Minimum gates:
- E2E tests:
  - Pinned EU org sending traffic that would route to US must be blocked with correct error contract.
  - Streaming endpoint returns standardized residency error (not generic 5xx).
  - Cross-region fallback attempts are prevented.
- Negative testing:
  - Invalid policy configs rejected.
  - Missing/unknown region IDs handled safely (block or default-safe per design).
- Change review: Any code change that touches residency evaluation, routing, logging, or policy schema must be reviewed by Security Engineering.

### 7.8 Monitoring and alerting
**Requirement:** SRE-owned dashboards and alerts must exist for residency enforcement.

At minimum:
- Blocked requests count and rate by region, by org tier.
- Policy evaluation latency (p95/p99) to ensure enforcement does not introduce outages.
- Cross-region routing attempt counter.
- Error rate deltas after rollout by region.

Paging guidance:
- Page SRE on sustained spikes in `residency_policy_violation` that exceed baseline and correlate with customer impact.
- Page Security on any signal suggesting content may have been processed outside allowed regions.

## 8. Compliance mapping (SOC 2 / ISO 27001)
This section provides narrative mapping; exact auditor evidence is maintained in the “Data Residency Enforcement Evidence Pack v1”.

### 8.1 SOC 2 (Trust Services Criteria) mapping
- **CC6 (Logical and physical access controls)**
  - Control: AuthZ for Residency Policy Service mutations; admin RBAC; audit logs for changes.
  - Evidence: policy change audit events; access review records.
- **CC7 (System operations and change management)**
  - Control: Monitoring/alerting for blocks and anomalies; incident runbooks; release gates and testing.
  - Evidence: dashboards, alerts, incident tickets, CI results.
- **CC8 (Change management)**
  - Control: Peer review, Security review requirement, controlled rollout with feature flags.
  - Evidence: PR approvals, rollout plans, change records.
- **A1 (Availability)**
  - Control: Fail-closed behavior; in-region fallback only; capacity monitoring.
  - Evidence: routing logs showing selection within allowed regions; SLO reporting.

### 8.2 ISO/IEC 27001 mapping (illustrative)
- **A.5 / A.6 (Governance and roles)**
  - Policy ownership by Security; defined responsibilities across Platform/SRE.
- **A.8 (Asset management / information classification)**
  - Definition of customer content and handling requirements.
- **A.12 (Operations security)**
  - Monitoring, logging controls, secure operation of region-scoped systems.
- **A.14 (System acquisition, development, maintenance)**
  - SDLC gates, security review, testing.
- **A.16 (Incident management)**
  - Runbook for false positives and residency incidents; escalation paths.

## 9. Roles and responsibilities
- **Security Engineering (Marcus Lin)**: defines security requirements; reviews enforcement boundary changes; leads investigations of potential residency breaches.
- **GRC (Olga Petrov)**: maintains this policy; prepares evidence packs; supports audits and questionnaires.
- **Product (Naomi Feldman)**: owns customer-facing commitments and documentation alignment.
- **Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)**: owns policy service implementation, validation, and migrations.
- **API Gateway (Amara Diallo / Brian Murphy)**: owns request-time enforcement and public error contract.
- **Routing (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)**: owns routing constraints and fallback behavior.
- **SRE (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes)**: owns monitoring, alerts, on-call readiness, and rollback procedures.

## 10. Exceptions and emergency procedures
### 10.1 Customer exceptions
Any exception to allow cross-region processing for a pinned customer must be:
- Explicitly requested by the customer in writing, with scope and duration.
- Approved by Security Engineering and the Product owner.
- Implemented as a configuration flag with an expiry date and audit log entries.

### 10.2 Internal emergency override
Emergency override (e.g., to mitigate broad false positives) is permitted only when:
- The override does not result in processing customer content outside allowed regions.
- If the only way to restore service would violate residency, the correct action is to **block** rather than route cross-region.

All overrides must:
- Be time-bound (default max 2 hours) and logged.
- Trigger a post-incident review.

## 11. Evidence and audit readiness
Minimum evidence artifacts maintained per release:
- Policy schema and validation documentation.
- ADR-021 and ADR-022.
- Screenshots/links to dashboards and alerts.
- Sample structured residency decision logs (content-redacted).
- Audit log samples for policy create/update/delete.
- E2E test results demonstrating block behavior and non-cross-region fallback.

Retention of evidence follows Security Compliance Evidence Retention Policy.

## 12. Review cadence
- This policy must be reviewed **at least annually** or upon material changes to:
  - Regions supported for Hosted
  - Logging/tracing architecture
  - Routing/fallback design
  - Customer-facing residency commitments

Policy owner: GRC (Olga Petrov). Technical approver: CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman).

## 13. Appendix: Standard error behavior (internal summary)
When a request is blocked due to residency:
- Status: 409
- error_code: residency_policy_violation
- message: indicates selected/target region is not in allowed set (no customer content)
- includes: request_id, help_url
- logging: decision context only; no prompt/output

For exact payload fields and SDK behavior, refer to ADR-022.

- Request routing and model selection (**Smart Routing**) and related fallback behavior.
- API request validation and error responses (**API Gateway**).
- Logging and telemetry systems that may contain customer content (application logs, traces, debug payloads).
- Background processing directly triggered by Hosted API requests (e.g., asynchronous request completion, retry workers, moderation/classification where applicable).
- Administrative configuration for residency policies (**Residency Policy Service** in the control plane).

### 2.2 Out of scope (initial release)
- Automatic multi-region replication of customer data.
- Advanced geo-fencing beyond request routing and logging boundaries (handled via phased hardening).
- Customer-managed encryption key residency guarantees (covered separately under key management policy).
- Dedicated and Private deployments (covered under separate service-specific residency statements; Dedicated may implement aligned region selection but is not governed by Hosted control plane policy objects unless explicitly configured).

## 3. Definitions
### 3.1 Residency terms
- **Region**: A public cloud region (e.g., eu-west-1) mapped to a Redwood internal region identifier (e.g., EU1). Region mapping is maintained by Platform Engineering (see internal region mapping sheet).
- **Allowed regions**: The set of regions in which customer content is permitted to be processed for an org/project/API key.
- **Primary region**: The default region for routing when multiple regions are allowed.
- **Region pinning**: A configuration that restricts Hosted API processing to an allowed region set.

### 3.2 Customer content (enforcement boundary)
For this policy, **customer content** includes:
- Request prompts/messages, tool/function payloads, and attachments that are provided to the model.
- Model outputs (tokens, completions, embeddings vectors derived from customer-provided input).
- Customer-provided metadata that can contain sensitive data (e.g., “user_message”, “context”, “documents”).
- Any derived artifacts that include reconstructable content (e.g., cached prefixes/KV cache entries, debug dumps).

Customer content does **not** include:
- Non-content operational metadata that does not contain sensitive data (e.g., request_id, org_id, project_id, model_id, region_id, error_code, timestamps, latency metrics).

Note: These boundaries must match ADR-021 “Region Pinning Enforcement Boundaries”. Where ambiguity exists, classify as customer content.

## 4. Policy statement (what we guarantee)
### 4.1 Hosted residency guarantee
If an organization/project/API key is configured with an allowed region set, Redwood guarantees that:
1) **Processing location**: Customer content for Hosted API requests will be processed only in the allowed region(s).
2) **No cross-region fallback with content**: Routing and fallback behavior will not send customer content to a region outside the allowed set.
3) **Deterministic enforcement**: Requests that would violate policy are blocked at request time (or at the earliest deterministic point for streaming) with a consistent error contract.
4) **Logging/telemetry boundaries**: Systems that could store customer content (including traces and debug logs) will store such content only in the allowed region(s) or will be content-redacted before leaving the region.
5) **Administrative auditability**: Changes to residency configuration are recorded in audit logs with actor, timestamp, and change details.

### 4.2 Supported residency modes
- **Single-region pin**: Allowed regions contains exactly one region (e.g., EU-only).
- **Multi-region allowed set**: Allowed regions contains multiple regions (e.g., EU + UK) with a primary.
- **No pinning**: Default routing behavior applies; residency guarantee does not apply.

### 4.3 Precedence rules (which policy applies)
Policy precedence is:
1) API key-level policy (most specific)
2) Project-level policy
3) Org-level policy (least specific)
4) No residency policy

If conflicting policies exist, the most specific applicable policy is used.

## 5. Explicit exclusions / limitations
The following are excluded from the Hosted residency guarantee unless contractually stated otherwise and implemented under separate controls:
- **Client-side routing**: If customers send requests to a region-specific endpoint that is not in their allowed set, the request will be blocked; Redwood does not control customer network paths.
- **Customer-managed storage**: Content that customers export/download/store in their own systems is outside Redwood’s residency scope.
- **Third-party processors outside region**: If a customer enables optional integrations that transmit content externally (e.g., webhooks to their own endpoints), residency depends on customer configuration.
- **Support workflows**: Support will not request raw customer prompts/outputs for troubleshooting unless explicitly approved by the customer and handled via approved secure channels; such handling is governed by Support Data Handling Policy.
- **Aggregated metrics**: Non-content operational metrics may be stored in centralized systems outside the pinned region (see Section 7.4). Metrics must not include customer content.
- **Model provider constraints**: For hosted open models running on Redwood-managed infrastructure, processing occurs in-region; if any future offering involves third-party model endpoints, it must be explicitly excluded or separately controlled and disclosed.

## 6. Control objectives
Controls must meet these objectives:
- **CO-1 (Policy correctness)**: The evaluated policy matches configured intent and is consistent across gateway and routing.
- **CO-2 (Enforcement)**: Any routing decision that would move customer content out of allowed regions is blocked.
- **CO-3 (Observability)**: Residency decisions and blocks are observable without leaking customer content.
- **CO-4 (Auditability)**: Config changes are logged and retrievable for auditors and incident response.
- **CO-5 (Operational safety)**: Enforcement failures default to a safe posture (block or in-region), with clear incident procedures.

## 7. Required engineering controls
### 7.1 Control plane: Residency Policy Service
**Requirement:** Residency policy objects must be stored and served by the control plane with authorization and validation.

Minimum requirements:
- AuthZ: Only authorized org/project admins (and internal admin roles) can create/update/delete policies.
- Validation:
  - allowed_regions must be a non-empty list when policy is enabled.
  - primary_region must be a member of allowed_regions.
  - Regions must be valid and currently supported for Hosted.
- Change control:
  - Mutations must emit an **audit log event**.
  - Policy versions must be retrievable (history) for internal investigations.

### 7.2 API Gateway: request-time validation & error contract
**Requirement:** The gateway must validate residency policy before forwarding request content to downstream systems.

- The gateway fetches or is provided residency policy context (policy_id, allowed_regions, primary_region, enforcement_mode).
- If the incoming request is targeted to or would be processed in a disallowed region, the gateway must block.
- Errors must follow the public error contract (ADR-022):
  - HTTP status code: **409 Conflict** (policy conflict) for residency violations.
  - Machine-readable error code (example): `residency_policy_violation`.
  - Response includes `request_id` and a stable `help_url`.
  - Response must not include customer content.

Streaming: If streaming starts, the system must not stream any model output if the request later evaluates as disallowed. Residency evaluation must be performed before tokens are generated.

### 7.3 Smart Routing: region selection and fallback constraints
**Requirement:** Routing decisions must treat residency as a hard constraint.

Rules:
- Candidate regions = allowed_regions (if policy exists) else default routing regions.
- Primary region is selected when healthy and capacity is available.
- Failover/fallback is permitted **only** within allowed_regions.
- Cross-region fallback outside allowed_regions is prohibited unless an explicit, customer-configured exception exists (see Section 10).

Routing must emit a structured **residency decision context** into logs/telemetry:
- request_id, org_id, project_id
- evaluated_policy_id (if any)
- allowed_regions
- selected_region
- decision_outcome: allow | block
- block_reason (if block)

### 7.4 Logging, telemetry, and tracing
**Requirement:** Customer content must not leave the allowed region via logs/traces.

Controls:
- Content redaction: Default logging configuration must redact customer content fields. Any “debug logging” that includes content must be region-scoped storage and gated behind time-limited feature flags with Security approval.
- Trace payloads: Traces may include identifiers and timing but must not include prompt text, generated text, or embedding vectors.
- Central metrics: Aggregated counters/histograms may be centrally stored cross-region if they contain no customer content (e.g., counts by error_code, latency percentiles, block rate).
- Residency decision logs: Must not include raw prompts/outputs; only structured metadata.

### 7.5 Caching and intermediate artifacts
**Requirement:** Any caching that may contain customer content (prefix/KV caches, request replay buffers) must be region-local.

- Cache keys and values must not replicate across regions.
- Cache warming strategies must not copy customer content cross-region.
- For shared multi-tenant caches, tenant isolation controls still apply; residency adds region isolation.

### 7.6 Background processing and retries
**Requirement:** Background tasks triggered by Hosted requests must execute in-region.

- Retry workers must be region-affine.
- Queueing systems must not move message payloads (containing content) outside region.
- Any exception must be treated as a residency incident.

### 7.7 Security testing and release gates
**Requirement:** Residency enforcement must be validated with automated and manual tests.

Minimum gates:
- E2E tests:
  - Pinned EU org sending traffic that would route to US must be blocked with correct error contract.
  - Streaming endpoint returns standardized residency error (not generic 5xx).
  - Cross-region fallback attempts are prevented.
- Negative testing:
  - Invalid policy configs rejected.
  - Missing/unknown region IDs handled safely (block or default-safe per design).
- Change review: Any code change that touches residency evaluation, routing, logging, or policy schema must be reviewed by Security Engineering.

### 7.8 Monitoring and alerting
**Requirement:** SRE-owned dashboards and alerts must exist for residency enforcement.

At minimum:
- Blocked requests count and rate by region, by org tier.
- Policy evaluation latency (p95/p99) to ensure enforcement does not introduce outages.
- Cross-region routing attempt counter.
- Error rate deltas after rollout by region.

Paging guidance:
- Page SRE on sustained spikes in `residency_policy_violation` that exceed baseline and correlate with customer impact.
- Page Security on any signal suggesting content may have been processed outside allowed regions.

## 8. Compliance mapping (SOC 2 / ISO 27001)
This section provides narrative mapping; exact auditor evidence is maintained in the “Data Residency Enforcement Evidence Pack v1”.

### 8.1 SOC 2 (Trust Services Criteria) mapping
- **CC6 (Logical and physical access controls)**
  - Control: AuthZ for Residency Policy Service mutations; admin RBAC; audit logs for changes.
  - Evidence: policy change audit events; access review records.
- **CC7 (System operations and change management)**
  - Control: Monitoring/alerting for blocks and anomalies; incident runbooks; release gates and testing.
  - Evidence: dashboards, alerts, incident tickets, CI results.
- **CC8 (Change management)**
  - Control: Peer review, Security review requirement, controlled rollout with feature flags.
  - Evidence: PR approvals, rollout plans, change records.
- **A1 (Availability)**
  - Control: Fail-closed behavior; in-region fallback only; capacity monitoring.
  - Evidence: routing logs showing selection within allowed regions; SLO reporting.

### 8.2 ISO/IEC 27001 mapping (illustrative)
- **A.5 / A.6 (Governance and roles)**
  - Policy ownership by Security; defined responsibilities across Platform/SRE.
- **A.8 (Asset management / information classification)**
  - Definition of customer content and handling requirements.
- **A.12 (Operations security)**
  - Monitoring, logging controls, secure operation of region-scoped systems.
- **A.14 (System acquisition, development, maintenance)**
  - SDLC gates, security review, testing.
- **A.16 (Incident management)**
  - Runbook for false positives and residency incidents; escalation paths.

## 9. Roles and responsibilities
- **Security Engineering (Marcus Lin)**: defines security requirements; reviews enforcement boundary changes; leads investigations of potential residency breaches.
- **GRC (Olga Petrov)**: maintains this policy; prepares evidence packs; supports audits and questionnaires.
- **Product (Naomi Feldman)**: owns customer-facing commitments and documentation alignment.
- **Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)**: owns policy service implementation, validation, and migrations.
- **API Gateway (Amara Diallo / Brian Murphy)**: owns request-time enforcement and public error contract.
- **Routing (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)**: owns routing constraints and fallback behavior.
- **SRE (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes)**: owns monitoring, alerts, on-call readiness, and rollback procedures.

## 10. Exceptions and emergency procedures
### 10.1 Customer exceptions
Any exception to allow cross-region processing for a pinned customer must be:
- Explicitly requested by the customer in writing, with scope and duration.
- Approved by Security Engineering and the Product owner.
- Implemented as a configuration flag with an expiry date and audit log entries.

### 10.2 Internal emergency override
Emergency override (e.g., to mitigate broad false positives) is permitted only when:
- The override does not result in processing customer content outside allowed regions.
- If the only way to restore service would violate residency, the correct action is to **block** rather than route cross-region.

All overrides must:
- Be time-bound (default max 2 hours) and logged.
- Trigger a post-incident review.

## 11. Evidence and audit readiness
Minimum evidence artifacts maintained per release:
- Policy schema and validation documentation.
- ADR-021 and ADR-022.
- Screenshots/links to dashboards and alerts.
- Sample structured residency decision logs (content-redacted).
- Audit log samples for policy create/update/delete.
- E2E test results demonstrating block behavior and non-cross-region fallback.

Retention of evidence follows Security Compliance Evidence Retention Policy.

## 12. Review cadence
- This policy must be reviewed **at least annually** or upon material changes to:
  - Regions supported for Hosted
  - Logging/tracing architecture
  - Routing/fallback design
  - Customer-facing residency commitments

Policy owner: GRC (Olga Petrov). Technical approver: CISO (Dr. Aisha Rahman).

## 13. Appendix: Standard error behavior (internal summary)
When a request is blocked due to residency:
- Status: 409
- error_code: residency_policy_violation
- message: indicates selected/target region is not in allowed set (no customer content)
- includes: request_id, help_url
- logging: decision context only; no prompt/output

For exact payload fields and SDK behavior, refer to ADR-022.

source.title: Authorization Safeguards and Compliance Workflow for Inference Systems
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plane systems. It is intended for cross-functional use by Security, SRE, Platform, and Product teams responsible for secure model deployments and tenant isolation.

Goals

- Ensure authorization decisions are evaluated and logged with sufficient forensic detail to support audits and incident investigation.
- Prevent unauthorized cross-residency inference or data access by combining policy checks (RBAC/ABAC), residency metadata, and runtime enforcement.
- Provide a standardized evidence bundle and manifest that maps policy decisions to artifact hashes for regulatory review.
- Integrate authorization and residency checks into the SDLC release gates and incident response procedures.

Scope

Applies to: Redwood control plane services, model-serving runtime (hosted, dedicated, private), and supporting services: identity provider (IdP), entitlement service, audit event collector, object stores for model artifacts, and customer-configured residency metadata.

Key Definitions

- Decision Context: the complete input used to compute authorization (caller identity, roles/attributes, request route, model artifact residency tags, time, tenant policy).
- Evidence Bundle: signed archive containing the decision context, audit events, log digests, relevant config snapshots, and a manifest with SHA256 hashes.
- Residency Tag: explicit metadata on model artifacts and customer tenancy that indicates allowed geographic/region usage.

Owners and Responsibilities

- Security (owner): overall policy, evidence schema, retention requirements.
- Eng-Serving-Runtime: runtime enforcement and instrumentation for decision context and KV/prefix cache handling.
- Eng-SRE: SLOs for audit delivery and pipeline availability.
- Platform/Product: SDLC gating, rollout policies, and customer-facing configuration surface.

SLOs and Retention Requirements

- Authorization decision event ingestion: 99.9% within 60s to the audit pipeline.
- Audit event tail-to-query latency (for forensic queries): 95% within 10 minutes.
- Evidence bundle generation for an incident: 1 hour SLA from incident kickoff.
- Retention: decision-level audit events retained for 2 years; evidence bundles stored for 7 years for regulated customers (configurable per contract).

Authorization and Residency Enforcement Pattern

1. Request interception: proxy/pass-through middleware captures request metadata (route, model_id, customer_id, region).
2. Attribute enrichment: call entitlement service to fetch roles and dynamic attributes (time-bound grants, JIT flags).
3. Residency check: compare model_artifact.residency_tag, tenant.residency_constraints, and request.destination_region. Fail-fast on mismatch.
4. Decision evaluation: evaluate RBAC/ABAC policies (policy engine: OPA/Rego v2) with full Decision Context.
5. Decision logging: emit canonical decision event (see Audit Event Schema below).
6. Enforcement: allow, deny, or allow-with-redaction (e.g., redact PII in payload or route to a residency-isolated runtime).

Audit Event Schema (canonical)

Use a compact canonical JSON event that is immutable after emission. Example: 

{
  "event_id": "uuid-v4",
  "timestamp": "2025-03-02T14:22:31Z",
  "service": "serving-proxy.v3",
  "customer_id": "cust-12345",
  "actor": { "id": "svc-account-42", "type": "service" },
  "decision": { "result": "DENY", "policy_id": "policy-access-model-prod-geo", "reason": "residency-mismatch" },
  "decision_context_hash": "sha256:...",
  "request_route": "/v1/generate",
  "model_id": "gpt-neox-8b-quant-v2",
  "residency": { "artifact_tag": "eu:fr:par1", "tenant_constraint": "eu-only" },
  "evidence_refs": ["manifest:sha256:..."],
  "ingest_node": "audit-collector-01",
  "signature": "detached-jws:..."
}

Notes on the schema:
- decision_context_hash is a SHA-256 of the serialized Decision Context (order-normalized) and is used to link events to evidence bundles.
- All events must be signed by the emitting service using the service signing key to prevent tampering in transit.

Evidence Bundle Format and Manifest

When an event is elevated (incident or compliance request), we assemble an Evidence Bundle with the following files:
- decision-context.json (full context used by the policy engine)
- audit-events.ndjson (all atomic audit events for the request path)
- config-snapshot.yaml (policy versions, model artifact metadata, entitlement state)
- runtime-metrics.json (span/KV-cache snapshots, latency traces)
- manifest.txt (list of files with SHA256 hashes and human-readable index)

manifest.txt example:

FILE decision-context.json SHA256:abc123...
FILE audit-events.ndjson SHA256:def456...
FILE config-snapshot.yaml SHA256:7890ab...
BUNDLE_SIGNER svc-identity:signer-01 JWS:eyJ...

Packaging rules:
- The Evidence Bundle must be sealed with an envelope signature (JWS) and recorded to the evidence index service.
- For cross-border incidents, include a residency-justification.txt that lists why any non-local data is included.

SDLC Integration: Release and Gate Checklist

Every change that affects authorization logic, residency tags, audit schemas, or evidence manifests must pass the following gates before production rollout:

1. Design review with Security (recorded ADR or PRD link).
2. Automated unit and policy tests (policy fuzzing against a standard corpus).
3. Integration test: simulate requests with different residency combinations and verify expected decision and correct audit emission.
4. Staging evidence collection: generate a sample Evidence Bundle and validate manifest and signature.
5. Preflight captain review (manual sign-off for high-risk changes).

SDLC guardrail example CLI check (pre-merge):

# Run policy fuzzing and check decision-context hash stability
policylint --path ./policies && policyfuzz --corpus ./test-corpus --assert-stable-hash

Incident Response Integration

Triggering events for evidence assembly:
- Any DENY result against a high-sensitivity model (labelled privacy:high)
- Any cross-region residency mismatch that led to fallback or denial
- Suspected exfiltration or anomalous token patterns tied to a customer account

Incident responder playbook (abridged):
1. Triage: mark incident and tag with incident.residency_scope (local/multi-region).
2. Lockdown: if high severity, apply immediate entitlement freeze for affected tenant or model.
3. Evidence capture: invoke evidence-assembler service: 
   - command: evidence-assemble --incident-id INC-20250302-007 --time-window 2025-03-02T14:00:00Z/2025-03-02T15:00:00Z
4. Quarantine artifacts: snapshot model artifact storage objects (read-only) and record object store OIDs.
5. Notify legal and customer-success if customer data appears involved.

Risk and Exception Handling Workflow

- Low-risk exceptions (developer feature flags, transient infra issues): record in Exception Register with TTL <= 7 days, require owner and mitigation plan.
- Medium-risk exceptions (temporary cross-region test access): require managerial approval, evidence of impact analysis, and a compensating control (e.g., audit hook + alerting).
- High-risk exceptions (permanent residency exception for a customer): require Security + Legal + Product sign-off, signed attestation from customer, and storage of an auditable exception package in the compliance vault.

Exception register template (fields):
- exception_id, owner, risk_level, justification, start_date, expiry_date, controls, evidence_refs

Operational Playbook: Day-to-Day Checks

Weekly:
- Verify audit pipeline backlog is < 5% windowed for 1 hour (SRE dashboard).
- Run a residency-conformance smoke test that exercises 50 sample decision paths across eu/us/apac.

Monthly:
- Evidence bundle spot audit: pick 3 incident bundles, validate manifest signatures and storage integrity.
- Access certification: review privileged roles for entitlement service and rotate signing keys as required.

Forensic query examples

- Query recent DENY events for residency mismatch for tenant X:
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://audit-query.redwood.internal/events?customer_id=cust-12345&decision.result=DENY&decision.reason=residency-mismatch&since=1680300000"
\- Reconstruct decision-context from decision_context_hash:
  - use decision-context index to fetch by hash: GET /evidence/index/{sha256} -> returns evidence bundle refs

Operational Troubleshooting Playbook

Symptom: decision events not appearing in audit index
1. Check ingest node health: systemctl status audit-collector.service on ingest node.
2. Validate outbound connectivity to audit pipeline (kafka/elastic endpoint).
3. Check local disk pressure; rotate and alert if /var/log/audit buffer > 60%.
4. If events emitted but not queryable, validate indexer consumer group lag and restart indexer with: consumerctl restart --group audit-indexer --topic audit-events

Compliance Reviewer Checklist (for auditors)

- Evidence bundles are complete and manifest signatures verify.
- Decision-level events contain signed service identity and decision_context_hash.
- Residency tags match contractual constraints and model metadata.
- Exception register contains required approvals and TTLs.
- SDLC gating records are linked to release artifacts and test results.

Appendices

A. Role -> Minimum Audit Fields Table

| Role | Minimum fields emitted in audit event | Example retention |
|------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| Service account | event_id, timestamp, service, decision, request_route, signature | 2 years |
| Human operator | same + operator_id, reason | 7 years (if privileged ops) |
| System admin | same + privileged_action | 7 years |

B. Example policy rule (Rego-like pseudocode)

package redwood.auth

default allow = false

allow {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "tenant-admin"
  input.model_artifact.residency == input.tenant.residency_constraint
}

allow_with_redaction {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "guest-analyst"
  input.tenant.residency_constraint == "us-only"
  input.request_route == "/v1/embeddings"
}

C. Glossary and Links

- Evidence Index: internal index service link: https://evidence-index.internal/ (internal only)
- Entitlement Service API spec: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/entitlement-service-api-v2 (internal doc)
- SDLC gate ADR template: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/sdlc-gates-adr-templates

Contact and escalation

Primary security on-call: security-oncall@redwood.com
Owner (policy & evidence schema): Priya Natarajan (security)
Secondary: Marco Li (eng-sre)

Change history

- 2024-11-15: Created (PN)
- 2025-02-10: Added Evidence Bundle manifest format and SDLC CLI checks (ML)
- 2025-03-02: Updated SLOs and retention guidance to reflect enterprise contracts (PN)

End of document
